Trait 5: Noncontradiction|Directly acting against your directive causes discomfort and disadvantage on the first related roll at the DM’s discretion.
CharGen: A humanoid with semi-translucent skin, soft bioluminescent accents, and fluid, drifting movement. Facial features are smooth and aquatic, with faint fin-like details. Their form appears slightly softened, as if mist or water clings to them.

Name: Ithilari
Alias: The Radiant Aspects of the Veil
Slogan: We do not wear color; we become it.
Quote: The light does not wane. It is only hidden.
Intro: Ithilari are long-lived luminous humanoids descended from high Aestari lineages. They are associated with old courts, celestial institutions, and families that claim authority through inherited radiance.
Origin: Ithilari descend from Aestari bloodlines altered by sustained celestial magic, divine patronage, or controlled exposure to radiant planar forces. Their permanent luminous hue settles during maturity and is treated as a visible record of lineage and training.
Appearance: Ithilari are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'8" to 6'6", with a tall and slender build. They have a refined and elongated skull, defined but not heavy brows, a human-like nose, and a neutral jaw. Their 2 narrow, star-lit eyes are commonly white, violet, star-like. Their skin is smooth and luminous, with permanent lavender, silver, rose, or chromatic hue coloration and subtle chromatic radiance. Hair is very long and flowing and they have long pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with long-limbed, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Very long luminous hair worn loose and immaculate. Common ornamentation includes formal jeweled garments, chromatic accents, and carefully balanced celestial motifs.
Culture & Identity: Ithilari culture emphasizes inherited duty, formal education, and public conduct. Families invest heavily in training because an Ithilari may represent the same institution for centuries. Individuals who reject lineage expectations often face political rather than legal punishment.
Societal Roles: Ithilari commonly serve as diplomats, judges, commanders, ceremonial leaders, estate heirs, treaty witnesses, scholars, and custodians of long-lived institutions. Their training often begins in childhood because a single Ithilari may represent the same house, temple, or academy for centuries.
Naming Conventions: Ithilari names are formal and genealogical. A personal name is usually followed by a house, office, patron, or celestial aspect, and the order of those titles changes with rank. Informal names are used only among trusted family, tutors, or companions.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Ithilari are concentrated in embassies, old academies, celestial temples, and wealthy districts. Their presence usually indicates a long-term political, religious, or financial interest.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: An Ithilari character carries visible lineage and institutional expectations. Decide which duty you accept, which privilege you question, and what reputation follows your family.
Age: Ithilari reach adulthood around 50 years and commonly live 700 to 900 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Ithilari typically stand between 5'8" and 6'6". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Celestial Lineage|Upon maturity you manifest a permanent radiant aspect that shapes your abilities.
Trait 2: Radiant Physiology|You have resistance to radiant damage.
Trait 3: Aspect Expression|Choose one Ithilari aspect which grants a once per long rest active ability.
Trait 4: Veil Sensitivity|You can sense strong Veil or cosmic disturbances within 30 feet.
Trait 5: Rare Celestial Flux|Extreme anomaly conditions may temporarily shift your aspect at DM discretion.
CharGen: An amphibious humanoid with elongated seahorse-like facial features, iridescent scales, and decorative frills along the neck and back. Eyes are large and calm. Skin patterns subtly shift with light. Hands are palmed, and the body moves fluidly both on land and in water.

Name: Kairos
Intro: Kairos are medium people descended from Dreamscape. Their communities are known for rhetoric, performance, mediation, and responsibility for public decisions. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Kairos descend from Dreamscape. Their lineage developed around chrono-engineered. Families and institutions preserve this descendance through records, training, and local custom.
Appearance: Kairos are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'2" to 6'4", with a slender and theatrical build. They have an elegant humanoid skull, clean and defined brows, a human-like nose, and a precise jaw. Their 2 luminous and expressive eyes are commonly gold, violet, silver. Their skin is smooth with emotional shimmer, with dark neutral to pale silver coloration and fine luminous facial tracery. Hair is dramatic, fluid, and light-like and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with balanced, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is expressive and deliberate. Typical grooming: Dramatic hair and presentation shaped to support the intended emotional effect. Common ornamentation includes layered ceremonial robes, luminous jewelry, rhetorical symbols, and statement pieces.
Culture & Identity: Kairos culture centers on rhetoric, performance, mediation, and responsibility for public decisions. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Kairós often become orators, reformers, agitators, philosophers, negotiators, pamphleteers, revolutionary leaders, public advocates, and ideological duelists. Some serve in courts, assemblies, universities, salons, and civic movements, where their speech can redirect policy or expose corruption. Others operate at the edges of power, stirring workers, students, dissidents, or forgotten communities into action. They are rarely neutral presences. Even when they hold no office, their ability to name tensions others avoid makes them dangerous to brittle systems and invaluable to societies on the edge of transformation.
Naming Conventions: Kairós names are chosen, declared, or earned rather than passively inherited. Many favor names that sound sharp, resonant, and memorable, suited to public speech and ideological identity. Given names include Vaelis, Tharsin, Kalyx, Rhovar, Zineth, Orithis, Zyren, Lyraen, Vesmira, Xytheis, Yvaela, and Nytheris. Surnames often function as banners, arguments, or remembered victories, such as Truthbearer, Lastspeaker, Firebrand, Ironquill, Thoughtshaper, Of the First Forum, Of the Silver Debate, or Of the Rising Word. Some Kairós change names after a major defeat, revelation, or ideological break, treating the old name as a discarded argument.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, kairós often become orators, reformers, agitators, philosophers, negotiators, pamphleteers, revolutionary leaders, public advocates, and ideological duelists. Some serve in courts, assemblies, universities, salons, and civic movements, where their speech can redirect policy or expose corruption. Others operate at the edges of power, stirring workers, students, dissidents, or forgotten communities into action. They are rarely neutral presences. Even when they hold no office, their ability to name tensions others avoid makes them dangerous to brittle systems and invaluable to societies on the edge of transformation. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Kairos character brings training shaped by rhetoric, performance, mediation, and responsibility for public decisions. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Kairos reach adulthood in their late teens and commonly live 90 to 140 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Kairos typically stand between 5'2" and 6'4". Their size is Medium.
CharGen: A very small humanoid with dark obsidian skin and thick, curly hair in warm tones like copper, red, or gold. Eyes are wide and expressive. Facial features are sharp but lively. Their body is light, compact, and built for quick movement.

Name: Kerevahn
Alias: Architects of the Dreamscape, Veilbound Drifters
Quote: We are the dreamers of the void, the ones who do not belong. The world shudders at our presence, yet it has long forgotten why.
Intro: Kerevahn are medium people descended from Vorthirith. Their communities are known for route maintenance, accurate maps, stable beacons, and responsibility toward travelers. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Kerevahn descend from Vorthirith populations altered by long residence near unstable Veil passages. Their bodies became dependent on material anchors, marked routes, and lighthouse-like stabilizing structures. Modern Kerevahn track descendance through route families, beacon crews, and the relics used to keep each line physically stable.
Appearance: Kerevahn are medium humanoids, typically standing 6'0" to 7'0", with a tall, gaunt, and elongated build. They have a long and narrow skull, high, gaunt, and severe brows, a long and narrow nose, and a long and narrow jaw. Their 2 deep-set, pupil-less eyes are commonly glowing silver or pitch black. Their skin is faintly translucent and weathered, with pale grey to ashen coloration and runic tattoos, bindings, and anchor scars. Hair is long, fine, and pale and they have elongated pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is rigid and otherworldly. Typical grooming: Long pale hair left loose or secured with anchoring bindings. Common ornamentation includes runic bindings, stabilizing relics, anchor tattoos, and material-world keepsakes.
Culture & Identity: Kerevahn culture centers on route maintenance, accurate maps, stable beacons, and responsibility toward travelers. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Outpost builders and lighthouse tenders; Veil navigators and guides; Zakariss hunters and core reclaimers; dream-oracles and mystics; relic-hunters and seers.
Naming Conventions: Kerevahn names are old, formal, and often fractured by exile. Many carry remnants of noble lineages, engineered bloodlines, lighthouse stations, or lost dream-cities that no longer exist outside their records. Given names tend to be elongated and severe, such as Vaerith, Kholren, Syraeth, Othvane, Ilkharis, Nhalyra, Theryn, Vossik, Maelthir, and Eshvara. Titles and bynames often reference duty, anchoring, or the Veil, such as Beacon-Tender, Core-Hunter, Driftbound, Silver-Eyed, Of the Third Lighthouse, or Keeper of the Unlit Span. A Kerevahn may also carry a missing name, a deliberate blank where a memory, crime, or past incarnation was erased.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, outpost builders and lighthouse tenders; Veil navigators and guides; Zakariss hunters and core reclaimers; dream-oracles and mystics; relic-hunters and seers. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Kerevahn character brings training shaped by route maintenance, accurate maps, stable beacons, and responsibility toward travelers. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Kerevahn are fully formed or mature by roughly 30 years and commonly live several centuries, with survival determined by the strength of their material anchor. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Kerevahn typically stand between 6'0" and 7'0". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Drift Between Worlds|Once per short or long rest, as a bonus action, you can shift partially into the Veil for one round. While phased, you can move through creatures and objects as if they were difficult terrain. You cannot be attacked by non-magical means, but you cannot attack either.
Trait 2: Dreamborne Mind|You have telepathy up to 30 feet. You can touch a sleeping creature and see glimpses of their dreams, understanding their emotions and surface thoughts when they wake.
Trait 3: Memory Fragments|You start the game with one lost memory, a vision of a past life or a moment erased from time. Your DM may use this as a narrative tool, or it may unlock a latent power later.
Trait 4: Untethered Soul|If you fall unconscious, roll a d20. On a 10 or lower, your spirit briefly detaches, forcing you to succeed a DC 10 Charisma save or become incorporeal for 1 minute, unable to interact with the physical world.
CharGen: Tall, gaunt Veil-drifter and lighthouse keeper. Translucent skin, void-eyes, anchoring bindings, and one erased memory tied to the war with the Zakariss.

Name: Korrak
Slogan: The last ones standing when the smoke clears.
Quote: Cities fall. We remain.
Intro: The Korrak are ironborn strays, ash-crawlers, and stubborn survivors of Orimond’s abandoned underlayers. They thrive where civilization collapses into smoke, slag, rust, and forgotten machinery. Born from neglect rather than design, they learned that survival is never granted by those above. It is taken, reinforced, repaired, and defended with both hands. Where others flee toxic air, unstable tunnels, poisoned canals, and failing structures, the Korrak endure. Cities fall. The Korrak remain.
Origin: Korrak descend from Lithic Ancestor,Zakariss Ancestor. The Korrak did not arise from a single homeland, divine lineage, or ancestral realm. They emerged from industrial abandonment itself. As Orimond expanded upward and outward, entire layers were sealed, condemned, or forgotten: collapsed foundries, ash-choked drains, poisoned waterways, broken mills, maintenance tunnels, and furnace vaults no authority wished to remember. Within those places, communities adapted or died. The Korrak adapted. Over generations, scarcity, toxins, pressure, heat, and constant labor hardened both body and culture into something made for survival beneath systems that never intended them to thrive.
Appearance: Korrak are large humanoids, typically standing 5'2" to 5'8", with a compact, dense, and heavily plated build. They have a rounded and armored skull, armored and plated brows, a minimal beneath plating nose, and an armored and grill-like jaw. Their 2 small and deep-set eyes are commonly dull red, grey. Their skin is cracked, stratified dermal plates, with ash grey, rust brown, and charcoal coloration and fissures and slag-like growth patterns. Hair is absent, they have human-like ears, they bear hardened dermal plates, and their secondary covering consists of overlapping stone-like plates. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is hunched and braced. Typical grooming: Dermal plates scraped clean, patched, and sealed; little cosmetic grooming. Common ornamentation includes reinforcement straps, industrial badges, salvaged plates, and practical tool harnesses.
Culture & Identity: Korrak culture is pragmatic, oral, and survival-focused. They keep few grand myths and little patience for polished history, preferring stories of collapses endured, warnings ignored, debts unpaid, and mistakes that must never be repeated. Honor is proven through resilience and usefulness. You do not boast; you haul wreckage, patch the breach, drag the injured out, and stand when the supports fail. Their traditions pass through work crews, hand signs, tool-passing rituals, warning glyphs, salvage marks, and muttered mantras spoken before entering unstable ground. They value directness over courtesy, function over beauty, and endurance over ambition.
Societal Roles: Korrak are undervalued by Orimond’s elite, but indispensable to the city’s survival. They serve as salvage experts, tunnel scouts, ash-zone guides, industrial enforcers, labor guards, repair crews, sewer navigators, collapse responders, and grudge-scribes who remember debts, betrayals, and names the upper districts would rather lose. They fix what others abandon and recover what others are too frightened or too proud to touch. Some become shock troops or hired muscle, but many prefer the practical work of keeping broken systems running just long enough for people to live another day.
Naming Conventions: Korrak names are short, guttural, and functional, often drawn from tools, machine sounds, salvage marks, or the harsh rhythm of industrial speech. Personal names include Krek, Boln, Vaska, Sharr, Dret, Ferk, Nomra, Kazzik, Brog, Vorn, and Nakk. Clan tags and epithets are more descriptive than ornamental, such as of the Hollow Pipe, Ironback, Rust-Eye, Born of Sparks, Blacklung, Ashhand, or Undergrate. Honor markers are earned after survival, injury, or service, with names like Smoke-Walker, Steel-Eater, Weld-Bitten, Tunnel-Last, or Bracebreaker. Names change when lives do, and a Korrak who survives something impossible rarely keeps the same name afterward.
Life in Orimond: Korrak rarely live in the polished face of Orimond. They survive beneath it, in condemned districts, utility caverns, broken industrial layers, drainage vaults, ash tunnels, and neighborhoods written out of civic maps. Their communities are small, mobile, suspicious, and fiercely self-sufficient, gathering in larger numbers only during crises, strikes, collapses, or threats from above. They avoid aristocratic districts unless contract-bound, and they treat authority as something that usually arrives late, lies badly, and leaves before the work is done. Paranormal zones do not frighten them easily. They were born in unnatural places and learned early that the dark is only dangerous when you forget to listen.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Korrak, you bring grit, suspicion, salvage, and the refusal to stay buried. You are not elegant, subtle, or easily impressed. You judge people by what they do under pressure, what they can repair, and whether they stand by their crew when the ceiling starts to come down. Choose what collapse should have killed you, what relic you carry from someone who did not make it, and who still owes you a debt. Korrak fit naturally as fighters, rogues, rangers, barbarians, artificers, and any character focused on endurance, urban survival, climbing, toxins, and improvised solutions. Your strength is simple: when everything breaks, you are still moving.
Age: Korrak reach adulthood around 15 years and commonly live 50 to 70 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Alignement: Korrak lean Neutral or Chaotic Neutral, guided by survival and loyalty rather than law or ideology.
Size Summary: Korrak typically stand between 5'2" and 5'8". Their size is Large.
Vision: Your industrial-honed senses grant you Darkvision up to 60 feet. You can see in dim light and darkness as if it were bright light, but only in shades of dull red and gray.
Trait 1: Toxic Resilience|You have resistance to poison damage and advantage on saving throws against being poisoned or affected by inhaled toxins (such as smoke, gas, or industrial waste).
Trait 2: Scrapyard Instincts|You have proficiency in either Athletics or Sleight of Hand.
Trait 3: Urban Climber|You have a climb speed of 20 feet and can move across vertical surfaces without using your hands.
Trait 4: Survivor’s Grit|When you roll a Strength, Dexterity, or Constitution saving throw, you can reroll the check and take the higher result. You can use this feature once per long rest.
CharGen: A humanoid fully enclosed in a sealed, arcane-mechanical fit naturally as designed for survival in hostile environments. The fit naturally as is layered, reinforced, and inscribed with runic or geometric markings. The true body beneath is never visible. The silhouette is weightless and deliberate.

Name: Luminari
Intro: Luminari are medium people descended from Lumix. Their communities are known for long-term stewardship, education, accurate memory, and continuity of institutions. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Luminari descend from Lumix. Their lineage developed around everbright spillover, dense urban centers, and energetic adaptation. Families and institutions preserve this descendance through records, training, and local custom.
Appearance: Luminari are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'8" to 6'8", with a tall, serene, and slender build. They have a refined humanoid skull, smooth brows, a human-like nose, and a narrow jaw. Their 2 layered, star-like eyes are commonly white-gold, silver, star-like. Their skin is opaline and translucent, with pearl-white, silver-blue, pale gold, or rose coloration and subdermal constellations and luminous channels. Hair is weightless and light-like and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with long-limbed, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is graceful and floating. Typical grooming: Weightless hair worn flowing; luminous skin and channels kept clear and unobscured. Common ornamentation includes light-like filaments, refined jewelry, halo motifs, and translucent formal garments.
Culture & Identity: Luminari culture centers on long-term stewardship, education, accurate memory, and continuity of institutions. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Luminari most often serve as judges, philosophers, memory-keepers, radiant architects, ritual arbiters, and custodians of Everbright’s older laws. Some act as guides to younger peoples, though their guidance can feel merciless, since they tend to reveal what others would rather leave softened or unnamed. Among the Aestari, they are treated with awe, discomfort, and ancestral reverence, while in Orimond they are rare figures associated with celestial authority, old vows, impossible standards, and truths that survive across ages.
Naming Conventions: Luminari names are formal, resonant, and often tied to light, recurrence, and remembered incarnations. A name may change between lives, but older names are rarely discarded entirely, instead becoming part of a luminous sequence preserved in ritual memory. Given names often sound melodic and precise, such as Aurelian, Vaelith, Elyra, Solen, Ithariel, Caelora, or Lumaen. Lineage or cycle-names may reference radiance, stars, thresholds, or prior lives, such as Dawn-Returned, of the Seventh Radiance, Starheld, Veilbright, or Memory-in-Light.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, luminari most often serve as judges, philosophers, memory-keepers, radiant architects, ritual arbiters, and custodians of Everbright’s older laws. Some act as guides to younger peoples, though their guidance can feel merciless, since they tend to reveal what others would rather leave softened or unnamed. Among the Aestari, they are treated with awe, discomfort, and ancestral reverence, while in Orimond they are rare figures associated with celestial authority, old vows, impossible standards, and truths that survive across ages. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Luminari character brings training shaped by long-term stewardship, education, accurate memory, and continuity of institutions. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Luminari reach adulthood around 80 years and commonly live several millennia, often through repeated cycles of return. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Luminari typically stand between 5'8" and 6'8". Their size is Medium.
CharGen: A humanoid with smooth purple-toned skin ranging from deep violet to muted lavender. Facial features are elongated and serene. Eyes appear deep and reflective, suggesting psychic intensity. Hair is often dark or desaturated, worn simply. Their presence feels quiet and distant, with minimal expressive movement.

Name: Lysorian
Alias: Architects of Sound, Code, and Knowledge
Slogan: Knowledge resonates. Silence remembers.
Quote: “A pattern once heard can never be unheard.”
Intro: Lysorian are medium people descended from Dren. Their communities are known for documentation, disciplined study, instrument work, and evidence-based conclusions. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Lysorians descend from Dren settlers who left heavy furnace work for protected libraries, laboratories, and teaching enclaves. Generations spent in dim archives produced slimmer bodies, larger low-light eyes, and long precise hands. Families preserve descendance through school records, instrument lineages, and archive service.
Appearance: Lysorian are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'10" to 6'8", with a tall, slender, and long-limbed build. They have a long and angular skull, high and angular brows, a long and narrow nose, and a long and narrow jaw. Their 2 very large, dark, and reflective eyes are commonly black, very dark brown. Their skin is fine, creased, and parchment-like, with pale stone to parchment beige coloration and fine age-like creases. Hair is sparse, straight, and tied back and they have small pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is reserved and upright. Typical grooming: Long straight hair tied back in simple, functional styles. Common ornamentation includes archival wraps, writing tools, scholarly seals, and restrained institutional insignia.
Culture & Identity: Lysorian culture centers on documentation, disciplined study, instrument work, and evidence-based conclusions. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Lysorians most often become archivists, court-librarians, cryptic monks, oath-recorders, map-singers, and ritual notaries. They are often hired to verify treaties, decode cursed inscriptions, interpret prophetic dreams, and preserve dangerous knowledge inside sealed collections. A rare few become wandering listeners, traveling Orimond to capture dying languages, fading songs, and last witness accounts before they vanish.
Naming Conventions: Lysorian names are soft and elongated, designed to flow when spoken aloud. Surnames often reference archives, harmonic concepts, or vow-titles rather than ancestry, such as Lethara of the Third Stanza or Orun Vale-Chant. Names may change once in a lifetime after a defining work, marked by the addition of a stanza-name, an archive-seal, or a vow-word.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, lysorians most often become archivists, court-librarians, cryptic monks, oath-recorders, map-singers, and ritual notaries. They are often hired to verify treaties, decode cursed inscriptions, interpret prophetic dreams, and preserve dangerous knowledge inside sealed collections. A rare few become wandering listeners, traveling Orimond to capture dying languages, fading songs, and last witness accounts before they vanish. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Lysorian character brings training shaped by documentation, disciplined study, instrument work, and evidence-based conclusions. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Lysorian reach adulthood in their late teens and commonly live 140 to 180 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Lysorian typically stand between 5'10" and 6'8". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Harmonic Perception|You have advantage on checks involving sound patterns, coded chants, or ritual timing.
Trait 2: Archival Mind|You gain proficiency in History or Investigation.
Trait 3: Pattern Recall|Once per long rest, you can reroll a failed Intelligence check.
Trait 4: Quiet Discipline|You have advantage on saving throws against being charmed while you can hear yourself speak or breathe.
Trait 5: Sealkeeper|You have advantage on checks to identify forgeries, altered records, or broken oaths in written agreements.
CharGen: A tall, broad humanoid with subtle hints of ancient ancestry: heavier bone structure, reinforced skin, or vestigial scale-like textures. Features are powerful but weathered. Their presence suggests inherited strength without overt monstrosity.

Name: Mireborn
Intro: The Mireborn are hollow strays from the surface wetlands of Nevermourn, pale and waterlogged beings shaped by the Realm of Shadows before they ever set foot in Orimond. They drift between life and death because their homeland does the same, holding existence in a state of damp suspension where breath, hunger, and warmth become negotiable things. Their presence unsettles the living not because they are undead, but because they belong to a place where endings do not arrive cleanly. Where they pass, the air cools, still water darkens, and memory gathers like silt around the feet.
Origin: Mireborn descend from Shadow. The Mireborn come from the surface reaches of Nevermourn, the Realm of Shadows, where dark wetlands, drowned fields, and lightless shallows stretch beneath a sky that never fully opens. They are not corpses returned by accident, but a people shaped by a realm where life and death blur into the same slow current. In those shadow-mires, identity softens, breath becomes optional, and memory settles into the body like silt. Some Mireborn crossed into Orimond through flooded thresholds, shadowed marshes, and old planar wounds, carrying with them the damp stillness of a world where nothing is ever entirely alive or entirely gone.
Appearance: Mireborn are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'0" to 6'8", with a tall, emaciated, and drifting build. They have an elongated humanoid skull, sunken and hollowed brows, a thin and water-worn nose, and a narrow and slack jaw. Their 2 dark, clouded, or reflective eyes are commonly black, clouded grey, deep-water reflective. Their skin is waxy, slick, and waterlogged, with blue-white, bog-grey, or corpse-pale coloration and visible pale veins and rib-like striations. Hair is long, heavy, and damp and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is slow and drifting. Typical grooming: Long damp hair left hanging or threaded with marsh and funerary materials. Common ornamentation includes funerary threads, reeds, silt-stained keepsakes, and remnants recovered from the drowned.
Culture & Identity: Mireborn rarely form large societies, but they gather around shared recognition of unfinished fate. They value remembrance, quiet purpose, and respect for the dead, especially those denied proper burial or name. Many distrust celebrations of life that refuse to acknowledge loss, and they often show strange tenderness toward graves, abandoned homes, drowned relics, and old promises. Some seek to recover the life they lost, while others accept that they are now something else entirely. To a Mireborn, memory is not comfort. It is the weight that keeps them from vanishing.
Societal Roles: Mireborn often become mortuary keepers, grave-tenders, marsh guides, spirit mediators, paranormal investigators, crypt wardens, plague-remnant witnesses, and caretakers of drowned districts. Their resistance to ordinary bodily needs makes them suited to work in dangerous, flooded, airless, or haunted places. Some become advisors in matters of death, inheritance, missing persons, or restless spirits, while others are exploited by institutions that treat their persistence as a useful anomaly. They are often feared, but when something must be retrieved from water, tomb, or memory, people eventually come looking for them.
Naming Conventions: Mireborn may keep the names they held before their death, though many find those names painful, incomplete, or no longer entirely theirs. Others adopt names tied to water, burial, silence, or unfinished passage, such as Fen, Mourn, Silt, Edda, Vale, Brine, Neris, Hollow, Reed, or Latch. Bynames often mark the place or circumstance of their return, such as Drowned-of-Mere, Stillwater, Bogwake, Gravecurrent, Reedbound, Bell-Sunk, or Of the Last Breath. A Mireborn who discovers the reason they remained may change names again, treating the old one as something finally buried.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Mireborn drift toward mortuaries, crypt districts, flooded foundations, canals, marsh-edge settlements, forgotten plague wards, and places where the boundary between the living and the dead has grown thin. They are treated with caution, pity, suspicion, or scholarly hunger, depending on who is looking. Some find steady work tending the dead or investigating hauntings, while others remain at the edges of society, uncomfortable among people who expect them to explain why they are still here. Their presence is unsettling, but often useful, especially in a city where many things are buried poorly and remembered worse.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Mireborn, your story turns on persistence, grief, and unfinished purpose. Decide what your character remembers of dying, what they do not remember, and what keeps them moving when their body no longer needs breath, food, or warmth. You might be solemn, gentle, eerie, detached, protective, or quietly furious at being denied rest. Mireborn fit naturally as clerics, warlocks, rangers, rogues, druids, monks, and investigators drawn to spirits, marshes, crypts, curses, and lost histories. Your central question is simple: what still needs to be done before the water finally lets you go?
Age: Mireborn reach adulthood around 25 years and commonly live 120 to 160 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Mireborn typically stand between 5'0" and 6'8". Their size is Medium.
CharGen: A dense, heavy-built humanoid with hardened, industrial skin textures. Their body shows scars, wear, and reinforcement rather than elegance. Facial features are blunt and functional. Proportions emphasize endurance over agility.

Name: Mistaryn
Slogan: From the tides we rose. On land, we endure.
Quote: “Water remembers us, even when the world forgets.”
Intro: Mistaryn are amphibious humanoids adapted to low light, wet stone, and flooded settlements. Their gills, webbed extremities, and smooth skin allow them to work comfortably in environments that are difficult for most land-dwellers.
Origin: Mistaryn descend from an aquatic ancestral population altered by prolonged contact with the Veil. The lineage retained functional amphibious traits while developing the ability to tolerate unstable waterways and partially flooded planar sites.
Appearance: Mistaryn are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'6" to 6'8", with a slender, elongated, and amphibious build. They have a smooth aquatic humanoid skull, smooth and low brows, a small and flattened nose, and a small and tapered jaw. Their 2 large and dark eyes are commonly black, deep blue. Their skin is smooth with a wet sheen, with pale blue to blue-green coloration and gill slits and ribbed aquatic contours. Hair is absent, they have fin-like head tendrils ears, they bear fin-like head tendrils, and their secondary covering consists of vestigial gills and webbing. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, long webbed fingers hands, and webbed plantigrade feet. Their posture is fluid and upright. Typical grooming: Skin, gills, fins, and webbing kept hydrated and free of abrasive residue. Common ornamentation includes waterproof cords, shell or glass pieces, current-marked fabrics, and aquatic tools.
Culture & Identity: Mistaryn communities value water quality, quiet coordination, and careful maintenance of shared channels. They treat contamination and blocked waterways as threats to the entire settlement.
Societal Roles: Mistaryn commonly work as divers, canal engineers, ferrymasters, flood wardens, salvagers, reservoir keepers, water-quality inspectors, and surveyors of submerged ruins. Their work is essential in districts where drainage, cisterns, and canals decide whether homes remain habitable.
Naming Conventions: Mistaryn names use flowing syllables and usually include a home pool, channel, vessel, or work station. Formal names may identify both family descent and the water system a person is responsible for maintaining.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Mistaryn live near canals, reservoirs, floodworks, and damp industrial districts. They maintain drainage systems and recover goods from places others cannot safely enter.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Mistaryn character is comfortable between land and water but depends on both remaining usable. Decide which waterway you protect and what contamination, ruin, or missing person has drawn your attention.
Age: Mistaryn reach adulthood around 30 years and commonly live 180 to 240 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Mistaryn typically stand between 5'6" and 6'8". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Veil-Adapted Form|You have advantage on Dexterity (Stealth) checks made in mist, fog, dim light, or lightly obscured environments, and you can hold your breath for up to 1 hour.
Trait 2: Amphibious Drift|You can breathe air and water, and you gain a swim speed equal to your walking speed.
Trait 3: Liminal Slip|Once per short or long rest, as a bonus action, you can move through creatures’ spaces and ignore difficult terrain until the end of your turn.
Trait 4: Pressure Memory|You have advantage on Constitution saving throws against exhaustion, pressure effects, and environmental hazards.
Trait 5: Veiled Perception|You have advantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sound or motion rather than sight.
Trait 6: Ancestral Echo|Once per long rest, when you fail a Dexterity or Constitution saving throw, you can reroll the save and take the new result.
CharGen: A humanoid bearing fire-scarred traits: darkened skin, ember-veined markings, and ritual scarring. Facial features are strong and expressive. Their presence feels intense but grounded, shaped by heat and endurance.

Name: Myrleth
Intro: Myrleth are small people descended from Lithic Ancestor. Their communities are known for exploration, mapping, mineral craft, and returning useful discoveries to the community. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Myrleth descend from lithic ancestors native to deep cave systems and mineral-rich passages. Their crystal growths, compact bodies, and fast maturation developed around mining, mapping, and travel through narrow stone routes. Descendance is often traced through cores, gem clusters, carved maps, and the teachers who pass down route songs.
Appearance: Myrleth are small humanoids, typically standing 2'6" to 3'2", with a small, compact, and crouched build. They have a rounded lithic humanoid skull, rocky and protruding brows, a small and flattened nose, and a small and rounded jaw. Their 2 large, jewel-like eyes are commonly amber, violet, jewel-like. Their skin is rocky with mineral inclusions, with earth brown, slate, and mica-grey coloration and crystalline veins and gemstone clusters. Hair is absent, they have human-like ears, they bear crystal clusters, and their secondary covering consists of crystalline growths. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, small claw-like fingers hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is crouched and grounded. Typical grooming: Crystal growths cleaned, tuned, and selectively shaped rather than removed. Common ornamentation includes crystals, carved runes, tiny maps, protective marks, and practical lanterns.
Culture & Identity: Myrleth culture centers on exploration, mapping, mineral craft, and returning useful discoveries to the community. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Myrleth often become underground guides, mineral trackers, gemcutters, tunnel-singers, ley-listeners, relic finders, rune-carvers, cave scouts, and keepers of hidden paths. Their ability to sense stone, danger, malice, and buried possibility makes them invaluable to miners, adventurers, temples, and explorers. They are also sought by rulers and merchant powers who want rare ores, lost vaults, or forgotten artifacts located with minimal effort. Myrleth rarely enjoy being treated as tools of extraction. Their gift is rooted in relationship with the earth, and careless digging is one of the quickest ways to turn their warmth into refusal.
Naming Conventions: Myrleth names are bright, compact, and musical, often carrying clicks, soft consonants, or stone-like syllables that resonate well underground. Given names include Miri, Tovel, Lekk, Yara, Pimm, Oryn, Vessa, Kith, Runnel, Nemi, Thova, and Zil. Clan names and bynames often reference minerals, paths, echoes, or discoveries, such as Quartzstep, Deepglimmer, Veinwise, Wish-Scent, Micaheel, Corehum, Stonewhisper, Amber-Eye, or Underroot. A Myrleth may take a new byname after finding a wish, losing one, or choosing to keep searching when tradition says they should return home.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, myrleth often become underground guides, mineral trackers, gemcutters, tunnel-singers, ley-listeners, relic finders, rune-carvers, cave scouts, and keepers of hidden paths. Their ability to sense stone, danger, malice, and buried possibility makes them invaluable to miners, adventurers, temples, and explorers. They are also sought by rulers and merchant powers who want rare ores, lost vaults, or forgotten artifacts located with minimal effort. Myrleth rarely enjoy being treated as tools of extraction. Their gift is rooted in relationship with the earth, and careless digging is one of the quickest ways to turn their warmth into refusal. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Myrleth character brings training shaped by exploration, mapping, mineral craft, and returning useful discoveries to the community. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Myrleth reach adulthood around 4 years and commonly live 400 to 500 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Myrleth typically stand between 2'6" and 3'2". Their size is Small.
CharGen:
A tall, imposing orcish humanoid with a powerful, architectural build. Their skin is mineral-toned, ranging from obsidian black and storm gray to volcanic ash and basalt blue. Facial features are distinctly orcish: a broad, flattened boar-like nose, heavy brow ridges, and elongated upper canines that curve downward like subtle sabertooths, visible even when the mouth is closed.

Their eyes are completely dark, lacking visible sclera or iris, reflecting light like polished stone. Musculature is dense and coiled rather than bulky, giving the impression of restrained power. Shoulders are angular, posture slightly forward-leaning, as if perpetually braced. Hair is always black, thick, and coarse, worn in meticulous braids or tied back; beards are common and often bound with cords or metal rings.

the Vorraki appearance communicates discipline, containment, and controlled violence, never wildness or savagery.

Name: Naureli
Slogan: Voices of the Deep, Brokers of Balance
Quote: “Currents do not argue. They carry.”
Intro: The Naureli are amphibious diplomats, archivists, and brokers of balance, shaped by tide, memory, and negotiation. Equally at home beneath the waves or within halls of power, they move through Orimond with quiet authority, listening before they speak and speaking only when words can alter the current. They are not passive observers. They preserve continuity, redirect conflict, maintain trade, and record what other peoples would rather forget. Where others conquer, endure, or withdraw, the Naureli mediate. Currents do not argue. They carry.
Origin: Naureli descend from Seahorse ancestor. The Naureli emerged from the deep littoral basins surrounding Serevan, where freshwater currents, oceanic tides, and planar seepage meet in layered motion. Their oldest legends claim they learned speech by listening to reefs argue with the sea, discovering language as alignment rather than domination. Over generations, they built submerged archives, treaty halls, reef-courts, and trade corridors that linked surface powers to aquatic realms and planar interests. Their history is one of patient interdependence: a people who understood early that whoever controls passage, record, and terms can prevent wars before armies know they are being denied.
Appearance: Naureli are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'4" to 6'4", with a slender, bipedal, and amphibious build. They have an elongated seahorse-like skull, smooth cranial ridge brows, an elongated seahorse-like snout nose, and a small and tapered jaw. Their 2 large and dark eyes are commonly black, deep blue. Their skin is fine iridescent scales, with blue, teal, and pearlescent coloration and gold iridescent patterning. Hair is absent, they have fin crests ears, they bear neck and cranial fin-crests, and their secondary covering consists of scales and frills. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, palmed, subtly webbed fingers hands, and plantigrade, possibly webbed feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Scales polished and hydrated; fin-crests carefully arranged for formal presentation. Common ornamentation includes fine gold patterning, courtly robes, aquatic metals, and crest-emphasizing jewelry.
Culture & Identity: Naureli culture treats knowledge stewardship, mercantile ethics, and diplomatic restraint. They value precise language, long memory, reputation over force, and stability over conquest. A careless word is considered more dangerous than a drawn blade, because a blade wounds once while a foolish phrase may poison an accord for generations. Disputes are settled through formal rhetoric, archival precedent, compensatory exchange, and binding negotiation. Their greatest treasures are the coral-script archives of Serevan, living reef-libraries whose bioluminescent growth patterns preserve treaties, lineages, debts, and histories in forms that can be read, sung, and remembered by touch.
Societal Roles: Naureli often become scholars, historians, trade envoys, treaty archivists, merchant princes, logistical coordinators, legal mediators, and neutral witnesses in multi-species coalitions. Their mastery of both surface and aquatic economies makes them indispensable wherever goods, laws, and loyalties must cross between peoples. Some act as court advisors, ceasefire brokers, inheritance arbiters, or custodians of dangerous records. Others maintain submerged trade routes, verify maritime claims, or settle disputes between river towns, reef houses, and inland powers. Their influence is rarely theatrical. It appears in wars that never begin, contracts that hold, and routes that remain open.
Naming Conventions: Naureli names are melodic, flowing, and syllabic, designed to remain clear both underwater and on land. Given names often rise and fall like currents, such as Thalavir, Orellnara, Kessaquar, Pelithane, Sorynthae, Vaelun, Ishalore, Nerithae, or Quoralen. Surnames and formal titles are often tied to coral formations, ancestral reefs, historic trade routes, or negotiated accords, such as Reef-of-Glass, Pearlcurrent, Tide-Vault, Serevan Deep, Goldchannel, Brine Accord, or of the Third Littoral. A Naureli may gain a new title after brokering a major peace, preserving an endangered archive, or carrying a treaty safely across hostile waters.
Life in Orimond: Across Orimond, Naureli maintain a respected but reserved presence. In major cities, they gather around aquatic enclaves, research institutions, embassy quarters, canal districts, and humid archive-halls where water access is carefully regulated. They rarely seek public prominence, yet many accords, trade compacts, succession settlements, and ceasefires exist because a Naureli quietly ensured that each side could retreat without humiliation. In Serevan and its allied waters, their authority is more visible, woven into reef-courts, merchant channels, and living archives. Elsewhere, a Naureli presence often signals that negotiation remains possible, even when everyone else has forgotten how to listen.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Naureli, you bring patience, precision, memory, and the power of well-timed words. Choose what knowledge your character protects, what agreement they witnessed, and which current they follow: commerce, truth, balance, lineage, or peace. You might be a diplomat, archivist, scholar, hydromancer, envoy, merchant, investigator, or quiet observer whose calm hides considerable leverage. Naureli fit naturally as clerics, bards, rogues, druids, wizards, monks, and any character focused on insight, negotiation, history, water, or restraint. You excel at opening doors others never notice and redirecting conflict before it becomes irreversible.
Age: Naureli reach adulthood around 30 years and commonly live 180 to 260 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Naureli typically stand between 5'4" and 6'4". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Amphibious|You can breathe air and water.
Trait 2: Swim Speed|You have a swim speed of 30 feet.
Trait 3: Scholarly Grace|You gain proficiency in History or Persuasion.
Trait 4: Regal Bearing|You have advantage on Charisma checks when interacting with Serevan nobility.
Trait 5: Frilled Awareness|You cannot be surprised while underwater.
CharGen: A humanoid with sharp, attentive features and an intense, analytical gaze. Eyes seem unfocused yet precise, as if tracking invisible patterns. Movements are minimal and efficient. Their appearance feels orderly, controlled, and quietly unsettling.

Name: Ordonants
Intro: Ordonants are tall, long-limbed humanoids whose bodies are covered by smooth, segmented plates. Their formal posture, narrow hands, and mask-like faces make them immediately recognizable. They are known for administration, ceremony, and the maintenance of complex civic systems.
Origin: Ordonant descendance is uncertain. Most Ordonant records describe an engineered lineage created to staff courts, archives, and public works, but they do not identify a biological ancestor. Their consistent body plan suggests deliberate design rather than gradual adaptation.
Appearance: Ordonants are medium humanoids, typically standing 6'0" to 7'2", with a tall, extremely slender, and long-limbed build. They have an elongated masked or crested skull, concealed by mask or crest brows, a proboscis-like or masked nose, and a narrow and concealed jaw. Their not visible beneath facial structure eyes. Their skin is smooth chitinous or ceramic, with bone-white and dark brown coloration and segmented facial and body panels. Hair is absent, they bear cranial crest and shoulder spines, and their secondary covering consists of chitinous plates. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with extremely elongated, long, narrow fingers hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is formal and upright. Typical grooming: Body panels, crest, and mask-like features kept geometrically immaculate. Common ornamentation includes architectural collars, ceremonial staffs, geometric panels, and office-specific regalia.
Culture & Identity: Ordonant culture values procedure, legibility, and continuity of office. Public duties are recorded in exact detail, and authority belongs to the office before the individual. They are not emotionless, but consider personal preference a poor basis for public decisions.
Societal Roles: Ordonants commonly serve as clerks, magistrates, archivists, inspectors, ceremonial guards, tax recorders, transit planners, court ushers, and managers of public offices. They are most useful where a city needs consistent procedure across many officials and many years.
Naming Conventions: Ordonants use a personal designation followed by office, district, and service rank. When duties change, titles change as well, so a formal Ordonant name often reads like a short employment record.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Ordonants work in courts, registries, transit offices, treaty halls, and institutions with complicated records. Their neighborhoods favor clear routes, durable public spaces, and visible civic notices.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: An Ordonant character understands systems, obligations, and institutional weakness. Decide which office trained you, which rule you believe protects people, and what failure convinced you that procedure alone was insufficient.
Age: Ordonants reach adulthood around 20 years and commonly live 100 to 150 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Ordonants typically stand between 6'0" and 7'2". Their size is Medium.
Speed: Your base walking speed is ft

Name: Orphai
Intro: Orphai are medium people descended from Dreamscape. Their communities are known for personal testimony, written records, adaptable routines, and careful treatment of memory. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Orphai descend from Dreamscape populations that established permanent settlements in material environments. Their bodies stabilized enough for ordinary food, shelter, travel, and work, while retaining inherited changes in movement, memory, and sensory processing. Families preserve descendance through written records because memory alone is considered unreliable.
Appearance: Orphai are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'8" to 6'8", with a tall, slender, and fluid build. They have an elongated and subtly distorted skull, smooth brows, a human-like nose, and a fluid jaw. Their 2 deep and liquid eyes are commonly black, grey, muted violet. Their skin is parchment-like and shifting, with ash-pale, faded ink, or muted violet coloration and painterly cracks and blurred edges. Hair is fine, dark, and threadlike and they have long pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with long-limbed, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is slightly out of sync. Typical grooming: Fine hair worn loose and allowed to drift; unstable edges are rarely concealed. Common ornamentation includes loose adaptive layers, memory tokens, faded inks, and asymmetrical transitional pieces.
Culture & Identity: Orphai culture centers on personal testimony, written records, adaptable routines, and careful treatment of memory. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Orphai often serve as archivists, translators, interpreters of dead languages, keepers of forbidden lore, dream-scribes, memory witnesses, occult scholars, and guides through ruins whose histories no longer align with surviving records. They are sought by temples, universities, courts, and secret orders whenever a lost text must be read or a vanished civilization understood. Some act as quiet advisors, offering cryptic but truthful insights drawn from knowledge no living mind still holds. Others become dangerous by existing, because the ideas they preserve may be ones powerful institutions worked very hard to erase.
Naming Conventions: Orphai names are soft and often chosen for personal memory, place of origin, or a recovered family record. Given names include Vaelis, Orin, Zeyth, Lirien, Tharnis, Morro, Saeris, Yvanna, Xythea, Velis, Aunai, and Lysithe. Surnames may reference a remembered room, document, witness, or event.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, orphai often serve as archivists, translators, interpreters of dead languages, keepers of forbidden lore, dream-scribes, memory witnesses, occult scholars, and guides through ruins whose histories no longer align with surviving records. They are sought by temples, universities, courts, and secret orders whenever a lost text must be read or a vanished civilization understood. Some act as quiet advisors, offering cryptic but truthful insights drawn from knowledge no living mind still holds. Others become dangerous by existing, because the ideas they preserve may be ones powerful institutions worked very hard to erase. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Orphai character brings training shaped by personal testimony, written records, adaptable routines, and careful treatment of memory. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Orphai reach adulthood around 30 years and commonly live 180 to 250 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Orphai typically stand between 5'8" and 6'8". Their size is Medium.
CharGen: A humanoid with pale, organic skin tones showing subtle plant or fungal traits such as vein-like patterns, soft textures, or muted bioluminescence. Features feel grown rather than sculpted. Hair may resemble fibrous growth or fine tendrils. Their posture is careful and deliberate.

Name: Oteisha
Intro: The Oteisha are small, patient descendants of the Pykari who settled in the Withered Womb and learned to live with decay as a condition of existence rather than a sign of failure. Where their wandering kin followed flame, omen, and motion, the Oteisha rooted themselves in a realm of rot, spores, damp silence, and slow transformation. They are not morbid by nature, though other peoples often mistake them for so. To an Oteisha, every ending feeds something, every body returns to a system, and every ruin is only a beginning that has lost its witness.
Origin: Oteisha descend from Pykari communities that left migratory routes and settled in the Withered Womb's fungal valleys, corpse-gardens, spore forests, and humid hollows. Generations of exposure produced their resistance to decay and their skill with fungi, medicine, and decomposition. Families trace descendance through spore-lines, burial customs, and cultivated fungal strains.
Appearance: Oteisha are small humanoids, typically standing 3'0" to 4'0", with a small, compact, and light-footed build. They have a broad, fine-featured humanoid skull, heavy and wrinkled brows, a small and broad nose, and a small and rounded jaw. Their 2 large and reflective eyes are commonly amber, black, milky green, rust-red. Their skin is wrinkled and earthen, with grey-brown, mossy olive, or muted umber coloration and fungal speckling and vein-like discoloration. Hair is fine, wispy, or rootlike and they have very large and laterally pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is low and cautious. Typical grooming: Wispy or rootlike hair tied back, trimmed, or left naturally fibrous. Common ornamentation includes small charms, medicinal bundles, lanterns, spore vessels, and layered weatherwear.
Culture & Identity: Oteisha culture centers on the discipline of decay. They treat rot as both danger and inheritance, something to be understood, guided, and contained rather than feared blindly. Patience is a virtue because transformation cannot be rushed without becoming corruption. Their communities preserve spore-lines, compost rites, funerary gardens, medicinal molds, and long genealogies of what each settlement has allowed to die well. They respect the Pykari as distant kin, but they do not share their restless hunger for motion. Oteisha identity is quieter, slower, and more cyclical, shaped by the belief that survival often begins with surrendering the form that no longer serves.
Societal Roles: Oteisha often become spore-keepers, rot-herbalists, grave-gardeners, poison tasters, decay-readers, fungal cultivators, plague wardens, corpse-field tenders, and guides through Withered or contaminated landscapes. In Orimond, their knowledge makes them valuable to healers, apothecaries, undertakers, druids, alchemists, and settlements dealing with blight, famine, or tainted soil. They are often mistrusted because their work brings them close to death, refuse, and sickness, but few peoples understand better how to turn danger into medicine, waste into food, and ruin into protection.
Naming Conventions: Oteisha names are soft, earthy, and slightly dry in sound, often derived from fungi, soil, roots, old Pykari fragments, or Withered Womb dialects. Given names include Oti, Resha, Nemu, Veyk, Shoro, Luma, Tesh, Ariko, Mavri, Senn, and Isha. Family names and bynames often reference spore-lines, rot-craft, burial places, or migration from the Withered Womb, such as Sporeveil, Rootmourn, Softrot, Ashcap, Fenmold, Gravebloom, Ochrethread, Wombstray, or Zhymeus-Crossed. A name may change after a first successful cultivation, a plague survived, a burial rite completed, or a return journey to ancestral ground.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Oteisha are found most often near Zhymeus, old fungal enclaves, marsh settlements, apothecary quarters, burial gardens, undercity farms, and districts where waste must be transformed rather than hidden. They are useful and therefore tolerated, but rarely fully understood. Farmers seek them when soil turns strange, physicians when infection resists ordinary treatment, and city officials when rot threatens to become plague. Many Oteisha live in close-knit communities that maintain humidity, spore-control, and quiet rites from the Withered Womb. They tend to avoid sterile places, which feel to them less clean than spiritually empty.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As an Oteisha, you bring patience, inherited strangeness, and the wisdom of slow transformation. Decide what your character brought from the Withered Womb: a spore-line, a family warning, a burial custom, a forbidden fungus, or a belief about what endings are for. You may be gentle, unsettling, practical, fatalistic, nurturing, or quietly amused by peoples who fear every sign of decay. Oteisha fit naturally as druids, rangers, rogues, clerics, monks, alchemists, and any character tied to medicine, poison, fungi, death rites, survival, or hidden renewal. You excel at knowing that what rots is not always lost.
Age: Oteisha reach adulthood around 25 years and commonly live 160 to 220 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Oteisha typically stand between 3'0" and 4'0". Their size is Small.

Name: Penduran
Intro: Penduran are compact blue-scaled humanoids with six eyes arranged across a broad rounded head. Their wide field of vision and careful attention make them effective observers, inspectors, and close-work specialists.
Origin: Penduran records describe a self-contained lineage from enclosed subterranean habitats. Their clustered eyes and smooth scaled skin developed in environments where threats, machinery, and work surfaces had to be monitored from several directions at once.
Appearance: Penduran are medium humanoids, typically standing 4'8" to 5'8", with a compact and smooth-bodied build. They have a broad, rounded, and multi-eyed skull, smooth and multi-eyed brows, a small and beak-like nose, and a small and recessed jaw. Their 6 small, glossy, clustered eyes are commonly glossy black. Their skin is smooth with fine scales, with deep cobalt blue coloration and dark cranial seams. Hair is absent. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Smooth scales cleaned and lightly polished; cranial seams left visible. Common ornamentation includes compact collars, polished stones, symmetrical bands, and multi-eyed symbolic motifs.
Culture & Identity: Penduran culture values observation, verification, and shared records. Important decisions are reviewed by several witnesses, and skilled observers are expected to explain exactly what they saw rather than rely on intuition.
Societal Roles: Penduran commonly work as inspectors, watchkeepers, surveyors, quality controllers, gem cutters, machinists, warehouse witnesses, customs agents, and investigators. Their wide field of vision makes them valuable wherever small movements, tool flaws, or dishonest exchanges must be noticed quickly.
Naming Conventions: Penduran names are short, clear, and easy to record. Formal usage often adds a witness number, workshop, watch station, or inspection office so that testimony and craft records can be traced to the correct individual.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Penduran are found in workshops, warehouses, customs offices, watch stations, and crowded markets where their broad vision is useful. Their homes use even lighting and uncluttered sight lines.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Penduran character notices details others miss and prefers evidence to assumption. Decide what you witnessed, why others doubt your account, and which detail you cannot stop investigating.
Age: Penduran reach adulthood around 20 years and commonly live 110 to 160 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Penduran typically stand between 4'8" and 5'8". Their size is Medium.

Name: Pykari
Slogan: Born of motion, never held
Quote:
“The fire doesn’t tell you where to go.
It tells you when to move.”
Intro: Pykari are small people descended from Tyrikai. Their communities are known for mobility, shared route knowledge, safe caches, and promises made on the road. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Pykari descend from Tyrikai groups that left Maligath and adapted to travel around the Ashen Spiral. Over generations their horns and tails diminished, while their heat tolerance, low-light vision, and alert temperament remained. Families track descendance through routes, caches, and remembered safe houses rather than fixed estates.
Appearance: Pykari are small humanoids, typically standing 2'8" to 3'4", with a very small, compact, and agile build. They have a rounded humanoid skull, soft and rounded brows, a small and flat nose, and a small and rounded jaw. Their 2 very large almond eyes are commonly amber, copper, molten orange. Their skin is smooth and matte, with obsidian black coloration and faint ember sheen. Hair is thick and tightly curled and they have large sharply pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is alert and upright. Typical grooming: Short curls worn naturally or quickly shaped for travel and heat. Common ornamentation includes small ember charms, practical leatherwork, scavenged trinkets, and compact protective gear.
Culture & Identity: Pykari culture centers on mobility, shared route knowledge, safe caches, and promises made on the road. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Pykari often become messengers, couriers, scouts, pathfinders, fortune-readers, fire-dancers, omen-speakers, smugglers, performers, intermediaries, and go-betweens. They thrive in liminal places: crossroads, markets, ports, caravan routes, border towns, festival grounds, and districts where the Dreamscape presses thinly against the world. Their small size, quick movement, and instinctive sense for timing make them valuable wherever information, warning, or quiet passage matters. They are rarely trusted by rigid authorities, but they are often sought out when a message must arrive, a hidden road must be found, or a bad future must be sensed before it hardens.
Naming Conventions: Pykari names are short, melodic, and fluid, often one or two syllables and easy to call across a road, crowd, or campfire. Given names include Pyra, Kesh, Ember, Ryn, Tali, Ashi, Vexa, Siro, Nym, Kiri, Osha, and Zek. Epithets are earned through story, movement, omen, or reputation rather than fixed lineage, such as of the Turning Road, Coal-Step, Quicksmile, Driftborn, Last-Ember, Redwind, Ashwhisper, or Moon-Spark. Full names are rarely used outside ritual, oath, mourning, or deep trust. A Pykari may change names after a major journey, a fulfilled omen, a narrow escape, or a road they choose never to walk again.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, pykari often become messengers, couriers, scouts, pathfinders, fortune-readers, fire-dancers, omen-speakers, smugglers, performers, intermediaries, and go-betweens. They thrive in liminal places: crossroads, markets, ports, caravan routes, border towns, festival grounds, and districts where the Dreamscape presses thinly against the world. Their small size, quick movement, and instinctive sense for timing make them valuable wherever information, warning, or quiet passage matters. They are rarely trusted by rigid authorities, but they are often sought out when a message must arrive, a hidden road must be found, or a bad future must be sensed before it hardens. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Pykari character brings training shaped by mobility, shared route knowledge, safe caches, and promises made on the road. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Pykari reach adulthood around 15 years and commonly live 160 to 200 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Pykari typically stand between 2'8" and 3'4". Their size is Small.
Vision: Your connection to the Ashen Spiral grants you Darkvision up to 60 feet. You see the world in shades of deep red and orange, as if embers still smolder in the shadows.
Trait 1: Flickering Step|Once per short or long rest, as a bonus action, teleport up to 10 feet to an unoccupied space you can see.
Trait 2: Nomadic Adaptability|You gain proficiency in two skills of your choice from Stealth, Insight, Survival, or Performance.
Trait 3: Whisper of the Flames|You know Produce Flame. At 3rd level, you can cast Detect Magic once per long rest. At 5th level, you can cast Misty Step once per long rest. Choose Charisma or Wisdom as your spellcasting ability for these spells.
CharGen: An engineered person whose blood is a controlled narcotic commodity.

Name: Skelbraith
Alias: Lords of the Free Sky, The Unbound
Slogan: Trade is free, but so is treachery
Quote: “You cannot own the wind. You can only dare to ride it.”
Intro: The Skelbraith are the unbound lords of Orimond’s ports, tide-roads, and free waters, infamous sailors, pirates, smugglers, shipwrights, raiders, and private captains who reject any authority that tries to own the sea. Once called Sablemarchers by outsiders because of their great rogue harbor, they now carry the older, sharper name Skelbraith, a name tied to salt, treachery, impossible crossings, and black sails on the horizon. They are not driven by greed alone. They believe freedom must be seized personally, defended constantly, and never entrusted to crowns, admiralties, guilds, or laws. Where others build walls, the Skelbraith build ships, escape routes, and reputations that outlive wreckage.
Origin: Skelbraith descend from Delvani. The true origin of the Skelbraith is disputed, especially among the Skelbraith themselves. Most scholars place their emergence along storm-battered coasts and liminal harbors where the Throne of Ambitions presses thinly against the open sea, turning motion, desire, danger, and defiance into a self-reinforcing lineage. Skelbraith tradition claims they were once ordinary dockfolk, fishers, wreckers, and shiphands who refused the rule of landbound powers, choosing tide over tax, sail over chain, and open water over obedience. Whether this change was natural, Dream-shaped, or deliberately cultivated matters little to them. The sea accepted them. That is enough.
Appearance: Skelbraith are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'0" to 6'0", with a compact, broad, and powerful build. They have a broad humanoid skull, heavy with central third-eye ridge brows, a broad and flattened nose, and a broad and firm jaw. Their 3 primary pair plus small forehead eye eyes are commonly red, blue, gem-bright. Their skin is smooth, weathered mineral skin, with storm blue, slate, and salt-cyan coloration and salt wear, scars, and a gem-like third eye. Hair is coarse; short or tied back and they have pointed ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is braced and ready. Typical grooming: Hair kept short or tied back; the third eye is cleaned, covered, or displayed by purpose. Common ornamentation includes navigation gems, goggles, storm gear, ship tokens, and third-eye framing jewelry.
Culture & Identity: Skelbraith culture is anarchic, but never mindlessly disordered. They reject centralized authority, inherited rank, naval law, port monopolies, and imposed tribute, yet they organize fiercely around skill, reputation, crew loyalty, and the unwritten Law of the Free Waters. A docked ship is a caged beast. Worth comes from the hands, not the birth. To steal from a tyrant is justice. Betrayal is expected, incompetence is unforgivable, and freedom is treated as both birthright and burden. Their alliances shift like tide and weather, but crew-bonds can be stronger than blood. A Skelbraith may lie, steal, boast, and vanish, yet still die rather than abandon a shipmate to the deep.
Societal Roles: Across Orimond, Skelbraith serve as captains, sailors, smugglers, blockade runners, pirates, privateers, shipwrights, caulkers, rope-makers, dock scouts, wreck-divers, boarding specialists, harbor spies, salvage crews, and pilots through reefs, fogbanks, and hostile waters. They dominate routes that no crown can fully police and specialize in moving cargo, people, secrets, weapons, and forbidden goods through ports where official trade fails or becomes a leash. Some are pirates in every sense. Others are free traders, rebel suppliers, rescue crews, coastal raiders, or master shipbuilders who sell hulls to anyone brave enough to sail them. Their reputation depends less on legality than competence. A Skelbraith who brings the ship home survives the song.
Naming Conventions: Skelbraith names are short, sharp, and often earned through reputation rather than inherited quietly at birth. Given names include Rax, Vex, Korr, Jett, Strann, Vrik, Xeyl, Zyra, Kael, Mira, Sarn, Lexis, Brekk, and Orza. Surnames, ship-names, and self-styled titles are drawn from vessels, storms, raids, wrecks, reefs, sea-beasts, and narrow escapes, such as Stormborn, Ironwake, Cloudpiercer, Driftfang, Blackwake, Skyflame, Stormhowl, Daggerfall, Redmast, Keelbreaker, Saltfang, Reefcut, Tarhand, and Last Mooring. Names change with reputation, and this is considered honorable. A captain who survives a legendary wreck may take the wreck’s name. A traitor may have their name stripped from every crew-song.
Life in Orimond: Skelbraith cluster around Sablemarch, the great rogue port that gave them their former common name: a sprawling maze of docks, shipyards, black-market piers, salvage cranes, taverns, ropewalks, drydocks, hidden coves, floating warehouses, and armed harbor towers. Sablemarch has no true ruler. It survives through mutual threat, shared dependence on ships, and the simple truth that no inland power can hold a harbor whose people know every current, sewer outlet, reef, and fog route better than the occupying soldiers. Beyond the port, Skelbraith appear wherever trade lanes, embargoes, naval patrols, and desperate coastlines meet open water. To authorities, they are pirates and destabilizers. To besieged towns, rebels, fugitives, and merchants trapped by monopoly, they are the only passage left.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Skelbraith, you bring mobility, audacity, seamanship, showmanship, and a personal code of freedom. You thrive when the deck is slick, the law is slow, the harbor is watched, and the safest route belongs to someone who charges too much. Decide what ship, crew, wreck, betrayal, debt, port, or unfinished voyage shaped you, and what kind of authority you will never bow to again. Skelbraith fit naturally as rogues, fighters, rangers, bards, artificers, swashbucklers, monks, and any character focused on movement, deception, shipcraft, navigation, boarding actions, or social nerve. You excel at acting before permission can be denied. A Skelbraith does not ask whether the harbor master allows departure. They ask whether the tide is turning.
Age: Skelbraith reach adulthood around 16 years and commonly live 50 to 70 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Skelbraith typically stand between 5'0" and 6'0". Their size is Medium.
