Trait 1: Third Eye Focus|Once per long rest, open your third eye for 1 minute. You have advantage on Perception checks and checks related to airship piloting, navigation, or targeting. While it is open, you can reroll one failed Dexterity saving throw.
Trait 2: Enhanced Mobility|You ignore movement penalties from difficult terrain. You have advantage on Acrobatics checks involving balance, climbing, or aerial maneuvers.
Trait 3: Aerial Reflexes|Reduce falling damage by an amount equal to five times your level. You can jump 10 feet further than normal. You can stand from prone using only 5 feet of movement.
Trait 4: Natural Showmanship|You gain proficiency in Persuasion or Deception. You can mimic voices and accents you have heard.
CharGen: A massive hunched horned humanoid adapted to cold climates with heavy ram-like horns, inward posture, and dense insulated build.

Name: Teleryn
Intro: The Teleryn are the nomad cousins of the Vorraki, broad-bodied warrior clans who move with herds, wagons, forgebeasts, and contract-banners across Orimond’s harsher roads. Where the Vorraki built small martial nations and leased disciplined armies, the Teleryn kept to clan routes, selling their strength as escorts, sellswords, caravan guards, monster-hunters, and battlefield auxiliaries. They are often mistaken for brutal raiders by those who judge only their tusks, weapons, and size. In truth, most Teleryn are deeply practical and surprisingly peaceful, preferring paid service, negotiated passage, and herd-life over conquest.
Origin: Teleryn descend from Dunavald,Maligath Ancestor. The Teleryn share ancient kinship with the Vorraki, descending from the same broad martial stock that learned to survive through strength, cohesion, and tactical discipline. Where the Vorraki settled into fortress-states and military charters, the Teleryn followed the open routes, adapting to migration, seasonal hardship, and the care of powerful companion beasts. Their clans grew around motion rather than borders, carrying portable forges, ancestral weapons, herd rites, and contract customs from one region to another. They became warriors for a price because war found the roads anyway, and because a paid blade can choose its terms more freely than a conscripted one.
Appearance: Teleryn are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'8" to 6'10", with a broad, tall, and powerfully muscular build. They have a broad and heavy skull, heavy and pronounced brows, a broad nose, and a broad with upward tusks jaw. Their 2 deep-set eyes are commonly amber, brown, dark red. Their skin is smooth to weathered, with rust-red to burnt ochre coloration and travel scars, tattoos, and ritual ash. Hair is coarse; long, braided, or locked, they have very large and pendulous ears, and they bear prominent tusks. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is low and grounded. Typical grooming: Coarse hair worn in braids, knots, shaved patterns, or practical road cuts. Common ornamentation includes clan tattoos, contract tokens, ritual ash, beast-bond charms, and travel-earned trophies.
Culture & Identity: Teleryn culture centers on matriarchal clans, contract law, road survival, and careful stewardship of useful creatures. Their small societies are led by elder women, herd-mothers, forge-matrons, route-keepers, and contract-speakers, whose authority comes from judgment, continuity, and the ability to keep the clan fed, paid, sheltered, and together. Strength is respected, but needless violence is considered wasteful, since every injury weakens the whole camp. Teleryn travel with bonded beasts such as Heart-Fur Yakul, which warm winter camps, and Ember-Crete Forgebeetles, which serve as mobile forges. These creatures are companions, tools, and clan obligations, cared for with the same seriousness as weapons, children, and elders.
Societal Roles: Teleryn often become sellswords, caravan guards, beast-handlers, outriders, camp wardens, contract duelists, mobile smiths, monster hunters, road negotiators, and mercenary auxiliaries. In battle, they are valued for discipline, endurance, and the ability to operate far from supply lines by relying on clan logistics and beast-supported camps. Outside war, they escort pilgrims, protect merchant roads, repair tools and armor, broker safe passage, and manage traveling markets. Their best warriors are not reckless berserkers, but professionals who know when violence is worth the price and when a strong campfire, a fed beast, and a signed agreement will solve more.
Naming Conventions: Teleryn names are bold, practical, and often tied to clan routes, beasts, contracts, or deeds of protection. Given names tend to be short and forceful, such as Brak, Torga, Vesh, Ruun, Ogra, Hettel, Marn, Korga, Daska, Voln, Shura, and Tek. Clan names often reference animals, weather, roads, or camp traditions, such as Heartfur, Redtusk, Yakul-Road, Forgebeetle, Ashhide, Longhorn, Embercart, Stormcamp, Ironmane, or Warmback. Earned names may mark a fulfilled contract, saved herd, winter crossing, or battle survived, such as Keeps-the-Line, Beetlefriend, Snow-Warder, Paid-in-Steel, Hearth-Tusk, or Three Roads Home.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Teleryn clans are most often seen at caravanserais, border markets, winter passes, mercenary yards, pilgrimage roads, and temporary camps outside cities that prefer their protection more than their presence. They arrive with beasts, wagons, portable shrines, hides, tools, and contract poles hung with tokens of completed service. Some cities hire them seasonally to guard routes or reinforce militias, while rural communities value them as traders, smiths, and monster deterrents. Teleryn rarely settle permanently, but their camps can become familiar landmarks, returning year after year with the same songs, beasts, debts, and warnings from the road.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Teleryn, you bring strength without cruelty, professionalism without servility, and the deep bonds of clan, contract, and companion creatures. Decide what your character travels with, such as a winter beast, forgebeetle, riding animal, hunting bird, pack lizard, or ancestral wagon, and what contract or clan obligation brought them into adventuring life. You may be peaceful until paid to fight, gentle with animals but terrifying with enemies, practical in negotiation, or deeply proud of never breaking terms. Teleryn fit naturally as fighters, rangers, barbarians, paladins, druids, beast masters, and any character focused on road-life, protection, mercenary ethics, animal handling, and mobile survival. You excel at knowing that a blade is only one tool among many, and usually the loudest one.
Age: Teleryn reach adulthood around 16 years and commonly live 60 to 80 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Teleryn typically stand between 5'8" and 6'10". Their size is Medium.
Vision:
You can see in dim light within 60 feet
as if it were bright light, and in
darkness as if it were dim light.

Name: Thornkin
Intro: Thornkin are living children of the Verdant Cradle, beings of bark, vine, root, thorn, and sap who walk in shapes that only partly resemble flesh. Their bodies change with season, injury, soil, and light, blooming, hardening, shedding, or sleeping according to rhythms older than stone. Some remain in deep groves and cradle-wilds, while others were taken, displaced, or stranded in the cities of Orimond, where their roots search for life beneath pavement and iron. A Thornkin carries the patience of growing things and the quiet threat of nature denied too long.
Origin: Thornkin descend from Verdant Cradle. The Thornkin first emerged in the Verdant Cradle, where life is not only abundant but sovereign. They grew from ancient root systems, awakened groves, blood-fed gardens, and places where memory passed through soil instead of speech. Some were shaped naturally by the Cradle’s will, while others were cultivated by forgotten rites or called into motion when forests needed hands, eyes, and voices. In later ages, many Thornkin were uprooted by trade, conquest, experimentation, or planar accident, leaving them to survive far from the living networks that once sustained them.
Appearance: Thornkin are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'0" to 7'0", with a tall, lean, and branch-limbed build. They have a root- and branch-formed skull, branch-formed ridges brows, a carved wood-like nose, and a root-formed and angular jaw. Their 2 recessed, luminous eyes are commonly amber, green, luminous yellow. Their skin is bark, roots, and vines, with pale wood to dark bark coloration and growth rings, sap seams, and moss. Hair is branch and vine growths, they have none ears, they bear thorns and branches, and their secondary covering consists of leaves, moss, flowers, and vines. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, root- and branch-like fingers hands, and rooted plantigrade feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Seasonal pruning, cultivation, grafting, and removal of harmful growth. Common ornamentation includes living flowers, moss, fungi, seedpods, woven vines, and cultivated seasonal displays.
Culture & Identity: Thornkin identity is rooted in cycles: growth, dormancy, flowering, decay, and return. They value patience, mutual dependence, memory of place, and the understanding that survival does not always mean motion. Many think of themselves through groves, seed-lines, seasons, or the land that first called them awake rather than through blood family. Displaced Thornkin often carry a deep ache for soil that knows their name, while urban Thornkin learn to treat cracks in stone, abandoned lots, rooftop gardens, and sewer moss as fragments of sanctuary. To them, nature is not peaceful. It is persistent.
Societal Roles: Thornkin often become grove-wardens, herbalists, boundary-keepers, root-speakers, wildland scouts, living shields, seasonal oracles, and caretakers of damaged ecosystems. In cities, they may become gardeners, reclamation workers, healers, smugglers of rare seeds, protectors of forgotten green spaces, or quiet saboteurs against industries that poison land beyond recovery. Their endurance and regenerative nature make them formidable defenders, but many prefer prevention over violence. When they do fight, they rarely do so in haste. A Thornkin’s wrath is slow to rise, but once rooted, it is difficult to remove.
Naming Conventions: Thornkin names often come from plants, seasons, growth patterns, old groves, or the circumstances of their awakening. Some use soft, natural names such as Briar, Rowan, Alder, Moss, Veyra, Lethwyn, Thistle, or Willowmere, while others bear harsher names tied to thorns, scars, or survival, such as Blackroot, Sapscar, Ironbloom, Winterthorn, Deepbark, or Redvine. Names may change after hibernation, fire, pruning, transplantation, or flowering. A Thornkin living among other peoples may adopt a simple spoken name while keeping a deeper root-name shared only with trusted companions or the land itself.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Thornkin exist uneasily among stone, steel, smoke, and regulated gardens. Some live in green districts, temple groves, rooftop orchards, and reclaimed ruins where roots can still reach water. Others wander the concrete wilderness, drawn to neglected parks, cracked foundations, drainage channels, and abandoned lots where wild growth survives without permission. They are often valued for healing, cultivation, and ecological repair, yet feared when their presence exposes how fragile urban control truly is. A Thornkin in the city is never entirely alone; somewhere beneath the pavement, something is still growing.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Thornkin, you bring patience, endurance, and the tension between rootedness and displacement. Decide what kind of environment shaped you: an ancient grove, a violent garden, a city crack, a sacred marsh, or a place that no longer exists. Your character may be gentle, alien, protective, stubborn, or quietly furious at what civilization calls progress. In play, Thornkin fit naturally as druids, rangers, clerics, wardens, fighters, and any character focused on resilience, healing, terrain, and seasonal change. Your presence asks a simple question: what will grow after the walls fall?
Age: Thornkin reach adulthood around 30 years and commonly live 200 to 300 years, with rooted elders sometimes surviving much longer. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Thornkin typically stand between 5'0" and 7'0". Their size is Medium.

Name: Tyrikai
Intro: The Tyrikai are small, sharp-eyed survivors descended from the ancestral stock of the Pykari, shaped by the cruel landscapes of Maligath rather than the open roads of Orimond. Where the Pykari became wanderers of omen, flame, and motion, the Tyrikai remained behind in a hellish realm of ash, hunger, predation, and hostile powers far larger than themselves. They are quick, wiry, pragmatic, and difficult to kill, thriving in the spaces between stronger peoples who mistake dominance for permanence. A Tyrikai does not survive by ruling the wasteland. They survive by understanding when to hide, when to bargain, when to bite, and when to vanish.
Origin: Tyrikai descend from demon-adjacent populations native to Maligath and share an older ancestral root with the Pykari. Those who remained in Maligath adapted to scorched valleys, predatory ruins, scarce water, and constant conflict. Families trace descendance through survival groups, salvage routes, and the horns, markings, and practical knowledge passed between generations.
Appearance: Tyrikai are small humanoids, typically standing 3'5" to 4'5", with a small, lean, and wiry build. They have a sharp and slightly elongated skull, sharp and arched brows, a small and pointed nose, and a narrow with visible fangs jaw. Their 2 wide-set and bright eyes are commonly amber, gold, violet, ember-brown. Their skin is rough and heat-worn, with crimson, rust-red, or maroon coloration and heat scars and dark striping. Hair is short, wiry, and dark, they have large sharply pointed ears, and they bear paired curled horns. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is low and ready. Typical grooming: Short wiry hair, horns, and tail kept practical for heat and sudden movement. Common ornamentation includes survival trophies, horn bands, scavenged metal, heat-resistant wraps, and clan marks.
Culture & Identity: Tyrikai culture centers on survival without surrender. They do not romanticize battle, conquest, or martyrdom, viewing such things as luxuries for larger peoples with more bodies to spend. Their settlements are temporary, hidden, and designed to be abandoned quickly. Trade, salvage, memory of safe routes, and careful reading of danger matter more than territory or grandeur. They value cunning, restraint, quick hands, and the humility to flee before pride becomes fatal. Among them, courage is not standing your ground against a monster. Courage is returning with food, medicine, and everyone still breathing.
Societal Roles: Tyrikai often become scavengers, scouts, trap-finders, tunnel runners, salvage brokers, spies, guides, poison tasters, night-watchers, and intermediaries between dangerous factions. Some become merchants who specialize in dealing with creatures much larger and more powerful than themselves, using flattery, deception, and practical value to survive negotiations that would destroy a less careful people. Others become shadow hunters or blade-bartered raiders, striking only when the advantage is absolute. In Maligath, the Tyrikai are often dismissed as lesser by dominant powers, yet they remain long after stronger banners burn.
Naming Conventions: Tyrikai names are short, sharp, and practical, meant to be spoken quickly and remembered under pressure. Given names often have hard consonants or clipped endings, such as Vrek, Xarr, Jal, Mesk, Tharn, Seka, Yza, Lyska, Nith, Oris, and Tazka. Surnames and bynames usually refer to survival traits, scars, professions, or hard-won reputations, such as Redsight, Hollowfang, Dustborn, Sharproot, Tradechaser, Clawbroker, Silentstep, or Ashweaver. A Tyrikai may change names after escaping death, betraying a patron, surviving captivity, or crossing into a new territory where old debts must be left behind.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Tyrikai are rare and often misunderstood, mistaken for Pykari, goblinoids, fiends, or refugees from some unnamed ashland. Those who settle in the mortal world gravitate toward markets, undercities, dock districts, caravan yards, and places where salvage, barter, and quick movement can become a living. They rarely trust permanent safety, even when offered it, and many keep escape routes, hidden stores, and alternate identities out of habit. To Orimond’s powerful, a Tyrikai may seem insignificant. To those who know better, they are valuable guides to Maligath’s logic: never assume the largest creature in the room is the one most likely to survive.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Tyrikai, you bring speed, caution, opportunism, and survival intelligence. You are not built to win fair fights, and you likely consider fair fights a trap invented by fools. Decide what your character escaped, what they learned to scavenge, and which larger power once underestimated them. A Tyrikai can be cowardly, brave, charming, ruthless, or deeply loyal, but their instincts are practical before heroic. In play, they fit naturally as scouts, rogues, rangers, merchants, spies, negotiators, and anyone who survives by reading danger before it arrives. Your greatest strength is knowing that outlasting the powerful is its own kind of victory.
Age: Tyrikai reach adulthood around 12 years and commonly live 70 to 100 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Tyrikai typically stand between 3'5" and 4'5". Their size is Small.

Name: Umbranauts
Slogan: Every road exists, if you know where to breathe
Quote: “The Veil is not exile. It is infrastructure.”
Intro: The Umbranauts are a people native to the Veil, the transitory expanse that envelops Orimond and connects distant lands and planes. Where most species treat planar travel as rare or dangerous, Umbranauts live within it as naturally as others live on land or sea. They are widely known as guides, navigators, and transporters, offering passage across continents and between realms with calm efficiency and practiced care.
Origin: Umbranauts descend from Ancient Veil Race. Umbranauts evolved within the Veil itself, a space where matter thins and distance loses meaning. Their physiology depends on particulate matter present only within the Veil, making long-term exposure to Orimond’s atmosphere dangerous or fatal. Rather than retreat entirely, they developed sealed traversal suits that allow limited interaction with the mortal world. These suits are not symbols of exile, but tools of trade and survival, as commonplace to them as boots or cloaks are to others.
Appearance: Umbranauts are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'2" to 6'2", with a slender beneath enclosed suits build. They have a soft humanoid skull, soft and subtle brows, a small and low nose, and a soft and narrow jaw. Their 2 large and dark eyes are commonly black or very dark. Their skin is smooth and pale, with low-pigment pale tones coloration and suit route and family markings. Hair is absent or concealed, they have human-like or concealed ears, and their secondary covering consists of fully enclosed pale veilsuit. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Bodies kept contamination-free; sealed veilsuits meticulously cleaned and maintained. Common ornamentation includes route, family, and contract markings stitched or etched onto veilsuits.
Culture & Identity: Umbranaut culture centers on movement, reliability, and trust. They value punctuality, clarity of agreement, and mutual respect between traveler and guide. Unlike many planar species, they are openly social and cooperative, preferring stable trade relationships over secrecy or mystique. Identity is often tied to routes traveled and services rendered rather than place of birth.
Societal Roles: Most Umbranauts work as Veil navigators, transporters, couriers, or logistical coordinators. They maintain stable passageways used by merchants, diplomats, and adventurers. Some serve as permanent intermediaries between distant cultures, while others specialize in dangerous or unstable crossings. A small number settle near Veil-access hubs in Orimond to manage contracts and waystations.
Naming Conventions: Umbranaut names are short, breath-soft, and designed to remain clear through veilsuit filters, sealed helms, and the muffled acoustics of planar crossings. Given names favor open vowels, soft consonants, and clipped shapes, such as Aesh, Vire, Luma, Kael, Thren, Oru, Senn, Isha, Vael, Nesh, and Thala. Family names exist, but they are rarely used outside intimate settings. In public life, an Umbranaut is usually known by a route-name earned through passages mastered, crossings survived, or travelers safely delivered, such as Crosswind, Longwake, Silver Span, Hollow Reach, Pale Crossing, Stillroad, Veilcut, Bright Interval, Salt-to-Star, Third Gate, White Span, or Near-Distance. Formal introductions often combine both, as in Vire of Longwake or Aesh of the Hollow Reach, because a route-name is more than reputation. It is proof that others trusted you with the distance between worlds.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Umbranauts are familiar figures near planar gates, arcane ports, and trade cities. They are generally well-liked and trusted, though their suits and breathing needs mark them as perpetually foreign. Extended stays require frequent returns to the Veil to replenish vital particulates, making permanent residence rare but not unheard of.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As an Umbranaut emphasizes mobility, awareness, and negotiation. You are comfortable navigating unfamiliar places and cultures, and you think in terms of routes and connections rather than borders. Roleplay often involves professionalism, friendliness, and an instinctive sense of distance, timing, and safe passage rather than secrecy or aloofness.
Age: Umbranauts reach adulthood in their late teens and commonly live 120 to 160 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Umbranauts typically stand between 5'2" and 6'2". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Veil-Dependent Physiology|You cannot breathe Orimond's atmosphere without a veilsuit. While suited, you are immune to suffocation and airborne toxins.
Trait 2: Veil Step|Once per short or long rest, you can teleport up to 10 feet to an unoccupied space you can see.
Trait 3: Planar Navigator|You have advantage on checks related to navigation, planar travel, or locating passageways.
Trait 4: Transitional Awareness|You cannot be surprised while conscious.
Trait 5: Professional Courtesy|You gain proficiency in Persuasion or Insight (your choice).
Trait 6: Safe Passage|Allies within 5 feet of you have advantage on saving throws against environmental hazards caused by planar instability.

Name: Unfaceted
Slogan: When the world looks away, we remain.
Quote: “We are not hidden. We are no longer permitted to be seen.”
Intro: The Unfaceted are a people whose identity was stolen so completely that reality refuses to resolve their faces. When someone looks at an Unfaceted, the mind attempts to assemble familiar features and fails, leaving blur, smear, censoring, or glitch-like distortion. They are not invisible and not intangible. They are present, audible, and physically real, but recognition slides off them like water off glass.
Origin: Unfaceted descend from Auctorion. The Unfaceted were once a named people with coherent lineage and history. Millennia ago, during a conflict that crossed planes and dream-architectures, their collective identity was targeted instead of their bodies. Their names were stripped from memory, record, and myth at the same time. They survived, but the world lost the ability to represent them as individuals. Over generations, the effect stabilized into a persistent perceptual failure centered on the face, and into a cultural adaptation centered on living without external validation.
Appearance: Unfaceted are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'0" to 6'6". Their bodies have ordinary humanoid proportions, but observers cannot reliably perceive or record their facial features. Eyes, brows, nose, and jaw may appear blurred, missing, or differently shaped to each viewer. Unfaceted use deliberate silhouettes, asymmetrical garments, veils, masks, and repeated personal motifs to make themselves recognizable.
Culture & Identity: Unfaceted culture centers on autonomy and absence. Names are treated as tools, chosen lightly and changed when life changes. Reputation is distrusted because it tends to become a hook that others can pull. They value memory, but prefer shared experience and oral witness over formal archives. Many believe fixation on permanence invites erasure. Community exists, but rarely through centralized institutions. Instead, they form small circles, traveling compacts, and caretaker enclaves in places already frayed.
Societal Roles: Unfaceted commonly become wayfinders through unraveling realms, caretakers of abandoned planes, intermediaries where identity is dangerous, scouts and witnesses who cannot be reliably traced, and residents of places other peoples will not remain. They avoid roles that demand public recognition, but thrive in work that requires discretion, endurance, and comfort in instability.
Naming Conventions: Unfaceted names are self-assigned and transient. Given names are often short and functional, and may be replaced after major events. Examples: Ash, Null, Echo, Reed, Solace, Veil, Static, Mira. Some adopt descriptive bynames such as Quiet-Step, Before-Name, or Of-No-Face. Some abandon names entirely while traveling through high-risk regions.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Unfaceted drift toward forgotten districts, sealed transit layers, condemned infrastructure, and neighborhoods that have fallen out of administrative focus. People may speak with them and remember the conversation but fail to recall the face. Records misfile easily, guards forget having checked them, and witnesses struggle to describe them in detail. This makes them valuable to certain institutions and dangerous to others.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As an Unfaceted means navigating a life without easy recognition. You may feel relief at being untraceable, or grief at being unremembered. You are comfortable in effrited places and uneasy in centers of curated identity. Choose what you do with the freedom of being hard to define, and what it would cost if someone truly saw you clearly.
Age: Unfaceted reach adulthood in their late teens and commonly live 120 to 150 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Unfaceted typically stand between 5'0" and 6'6". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Faceless Cognition|Creatures have disadvantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks to identify you from visual description, sketches, or secondhand accounts.
Trait 2: Slip in Recall|After a creature speaks with you for at least 1 minute, it must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw (DC 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier) or forget one noncritical visual detail about you for 24 hours.
Trait 3: This Is Not A Face|Once per long rest, when you are targeted by an attack you can impose disadvantage on that attack as the attacker’s perception stutters.
Trait 4: Unravel Wayfinder|You have advantage on Survival and Investigation checks to navigate, map, or interpret unstable, effrited, or unraveling environments and planar anomalies.

Name: Vaelari
Alias: The Veil-Touched, Children of the Second Sight
Slogan: Thought shapes reality, and reality listens.
Quote: “We do not hear voices. We hear echoes that have not yet learned silence.”
Intro: The Vaelari are a reclusive and enigmatic people, marked by violet skin and an ever-present psychic resonance. Descended from the Aestari yet profoundly altered by generations of exposure to psychic currents and paranormal forces, they walk the edge between mind and matter. To most folk of Orimond, the Vaelari feel unsettling not because they are hostile, but because they seem to know things that have not yet been spoken.
Origin: Vaelari descend from Aestari. The Varnhallans arose in the shadow of ancient stoneworks and buried foundations left by the Moeder, an extinct precursor people whose ruins still shape parts of Orimond. Though the Moeder vanished long ago, their legacy lingers in Varnhallan resilience, structural instinct, and almost bodily understanding of load, stress, and support. Early Varnhallan communities settled among these remnants, adapting them into furnace-halls, citadels, foundries, and defensible settlements of fire and iron. Over generations, they became a people who do not only inhabit structures. They inherit, test, rebuild, and strengthen them.
Appearance: Vaelari are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'6" to 6'2", with a tall, slender, and athletic build. They have an elongated and refined skull, smooth and swept into horn base brows, a human-like and narrow nose, and a narrow jaw. Their 2 large, black or luminous eyes are commonly black, violet, softly luminous. Their skin is smooth, with lavender to deep amethyst coloration and faint psychic motes and facial ridges. Hair is absent or minimal, they have long pointed ears, and they bear paired swept cranial horns. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with long-limbed, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Hairless or closely kept; horns and smooth skin surfaces carefully maintained. Common ornamentation includes sleek dark garments, horn accents, psychic-light jewelry, and minimal geometric pieces.
Culture & Identity: Vaelari society values perception, restraint, and mental discipline. Emotion is not suppressed, but studied, understood, and guided. They believe that unchecked thought can wound the world as surely as steel. Personal identity is fluid, shaped by memory, vision, and shared psychic experience rather than rigid hierarchy. Privacy of thought is sacred, and violating another’s mind is considered one of the gravest taboos.
Societal Roles: Vaelari most often serve as seers, psychic mediators, scholars of the unseen, and wardens of anomalous sites. Some act as advisors to rulers who dare to consult them, while others wander Orimond investigating disturbances of mind, dream, or spirit. A rare few become battle-psions, turning focused will into devastating force when restraint fails.
Naming Conventions: Umbranaut names are short, breath-soft, and shaped to remain clear through veilsuit filters, pressure membranes, and distorted planar acoustics. Given names often favor open vowels, soft consonants, and clipped endings, such as Aesh, Vire, Luma, Kael, Thren, Oru, Senn, Vael, Iri, Nesh, and Thala. Family names are less important than route-names, which are earned through passages mastered, crossings survived, or contracts fulfilled. Examples include Crosswind, Longwake, Silver Span, Hollow Reach, Pale Crossing, Stillroad, Veilcut, Bright Interval, Salt-to-Star, and Third Gate. Formal introductions often include both name and route, as in Vire of Longwake or Aesh, keeper of the Hollow Reach. Among Umbranauts, a route-name is more than reputation. It is proof that others trusted you with the distance between worlds.
Life in Orimond: In the mortal world, Vaelari communities are small and intentionally distant, often built near places of psychic resonance or thin Veil boundaries. They are tolerated, sometimes sought out, and occasionally feared. Daily life requires constant mental grounding, as the psychic noise of dense cities can be overwhelming. Many Vaelari leave their enclaves only briefly, returning once their senses grow strained.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Vaelari, you bring awareness, restraint, and internal conflict. You may sense truths others ignore, struggle with intrusive visions, or feel responsible for dangers no one else perceives. Decide how your character balances empathy with detachment, and whether they fear losing themselves to the thoughts they touch.
Age: Vaelari reach adulthood in their late teens and commonly live 90 to 120 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Alignement: Typically Neutral or Neutral Good, guided more by perception and consequence than rigid moral codes.
Size Summary: Vaelari typically stand between 5'6" and 6'2". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Psychic Sensitivity|You have advantage on Insight checks and saving throws against being charmed.
Trait 2: Mind Whisper|You can communicate telepathically with a creature you can see within 30 feet.
Trait 3: Veil Awareness|You can sense active psychic or paranormal effects within 30 feet.
Trait 4: Focused Will|Once per long rest, you have advantage on one Intelligence or Wisdom saving throw.
Trait 5: Mental Discipline|You have resistance to psychic damage.
Trait 6: Echo of Thought|Once per long rest, you can reroll a failed mental ability check.

Name: Varnhallan
Slogan: What endures is what is forged.
Quote: “Stone remembers. Steel obeys.”
Intro: The Varnhallans are the ironborn of Varnhallow, a people shaped by labor, heat, stone, and unrelenting purpose. Born in furnace-cities, mountain holds, and forge-settlements, they thrive where others falter, treating smoke, pressure, exhaustion, and danger as part of the work rather than reasons to stop. They are builders, wardens, smiths, and survivors, less concerned with conquest than with making things that endure. To a Varnhallan, progress is not declared. It is forged, tested, repaired, and struck again until it holds.
Origin: Varnhallan descend from Delvani, Moeder. Varnhallians arose in the shadow of ancient stoneworks and forgotten foundations once raised by the Moeder, an extinct precursor race. Though the Moeder are long gone, fragments of their legacy linger in Varnhallian resilience and instinctive understanding of structure and load. Over generations, the Varnhallians became a people of foundries, citadels, and deep halls, adapting Moeder remnants into living cities of fire and iron.
Appearance: Varnhallan are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'6" to 6'6", with a broad-shouldered, dense, and heavy build. They have a strong humanoid skull, strong and heavy brows, a broad and straight nose, and a broad and grounded jaw. Their 2 deep-set eyes are commonly steel-grey, copper, dark iron, ember-gold. Their skin is roughened and callused, with ash-pale, bronze, iron-brown, or forge-dark coloration and scars, heat exposure, and work marks. Hair is thick, coarse, and tightly bound and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is grounded and braced. Typical grooming: Thick hair kept short or tightly bound away from tools, stone dust, and flame. Common ornamentation includes work leathers, guild insignia, medallions, tool badges, and earned craft marks.
Culture & Identity: Varnhallan culture values duty, endurance, and proven skill above reputation or inheritance. Words are treated as raw ore until work gives them weight. Tradition matters, but only if it survives testing; anything untested remains suspect, no matter how ancient. They believe structures, tools, plans, and people must be stressed to reveal their worth, and that failure is acceptable only when studied, repaired, and remembered. Their independence often reads as stubbornness, but to them it is responsibility. A thing that holds only because someone else commands it was never strong to begin with.
Societal Roles: Varnhallans often become smiths, masons, siege engineers, heavy infantry, wardens of fortified cities, ruin-keepers, toolwrights, bridge-builders, and defenders of mountain holds. In war, they form the unyielding core of defensive lines, holding gates, walls, and chokepoints long after retreat would be reasonable. In peace, they build and maintain what other peoples rely on but rarely understand: walls, aqueducts, locks, foundries, arsenals, roads, and deep foundations. They are often called when something must hold under pressure, whether that thing is a fortress, a treaty, a weapon, or a failing city.
Naming Conventions: Varnhallan names are short, heavy, and practical, made to be spoken clearly in forge-halls, barracks, quarries, and storming yards. Given names include Rurik, Bran, Durn, Othar, Kaela, Brenna, Harn, Vorka, Mael, Sigrun, Tova, and Keld. Surnames often reference craft, material, endurance, or defensive works, such as Stoneforge, Emberhand, Ironvein, Deepwall, Hammerfall, Gatebraid, Ashmantle, Steelroot, Blackkiln, and Holdfast. Earned names may be added after a siege, masterwork, collapse survived, or oath kept under impossible pressure.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Varnhallans gather in fortified districts, forge-settlements, mountain holds, industrial wards, military foundries, and old Moeder-adjacent ruins where craft and defense are inseparable. In mixed societies, they are valued as infrastructure keepers, siege experts, builders, and reliable defenders, but often distrusted for their stubborn independence and refusal to flatter fragile authority. Their neighborhoods tend to be functional, reinforced, and loud with work: hammer-song, stone-cutting, bellows, shouted measures, and the dull rhythm of things being made to last. When a Varnhallan district goes silent, something is wrong.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Varnhallan, you bring discipline, resilience, craft, and the refusal to yield before the work is done. You may be a defender, mason, smith, soldier, ruin-warden, siege engineer, or practical wanderer seeking materials, methods, or lost Moeder secrets. Decide what you have built, what you failed to hold, and what test you believe will reveal your true worth. Varnhallans fit naturally as fighters, paladins, artificers, clerics, rangers, and any character focused on defense, tools, structure, endurance, and deliberate action. You excel at consistency rather than flourish. When others retreat, you brace.
Age: Varnhallan reach adulthood around 20 years and commonly live 100 to 120 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Varnhallan typically stand between 5'6" and 6'6". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Forge Endurance|Once per long rest, reduce damage taken by 1d12 + Constitution modifier.
Trait 2: Structural Instinct|You have advantage on checks to assess fortifications, buildings, or stonework.
Trait 3: Heat-Hardened|You have resistance to fire damage.
Trait 4: Immovable|You have advantage on saving throws against being pushed or knocked prone.
Trait 5: Tool Proficiency|Proficiency with Smith’s Tools or Mason’s Tools.
Trait 6: Last to Yield|When reduced to 0 hit points but not killed outright, you drop to 1 hit points instead (once per long rest).

Name: Veilborn
Intro: Veilborn are medium people descended from Ancient Veil Race,Moeder. Their communities are known for bodily stabilization, mutual aid, specialist medicine, and reliable personal records. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Veilborn descend from Ancient Veil peoples and Moeder-descended settlers who lived near persistent breaches for many generations. Children inherited a stable humanoid frame along with four arms, translucent tissue, and fragment-like growths. Descendance is tracked through clinic records, stabilizing garments, and families that share similar breach exposure.
Appearance: Veilborn are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'4" to 7'0", with a tall, fragmented, and unstable build. They have a humanoid but partially dissolved skull, fragmented and partially dissolved brows, an unstable or obscured nose, and a fragmented and unstable jaw. Their 2 void-like or washed-out eyes are commonly void black, washed-out white, cyan. Their skin is translucent and glass-like, with pale spectral with prismatic fragments coloration and spectral veins and broken reflective planes. Hair is absent, they bear floating shard-like protrusions, and their secondary covering consists of prismatic fragments and trailing veil matter. They have 4 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, long fragmented fingers hands, and hovering; feet vestigial or fragmented feet. Their posture is hovering and misaligned. Typical grooming: Unstable fragments aligned or bound where possible; little conventional grooming. Common ornamentation includes anchoring ribbons, prismatic shards, stabilizing sigils, and objects that preserve identity.
Culture & Identity: Veilborn culture centers on bodily stabilization, mutual aid, specialist medicine, and reliable personal records. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Veilborn often become scouts, infiltrators, memory-readers, guides through anomaly zones, occult witnesses, couriers, and keepers of forgotten routes. Their unstable nature makes them difficult to track, difficult to confine, and unusually suited to work in places where normal bodies and records fail. Some serve underground networks as unseen messengers or wardens of abandoned passages, while others are coerced by corporations, governments, or occult orders seeking to weaponize their condition. A Veilborn’s value often comes from the same quality that makes others fear them: sometimes they are present exactly where no one expects presence to be possible.
Naming Conventions: Veilborn names are often chosen after manifestation, especially when old names feel incomplete, dangerous, or no longer true. Some keep ordinary names as anchors to the life they remember, while others adopt short symbolic names such as Static, Vale, Echo, Null, Flicker, Haze, Wisp, or Trace. Bynames often refer to the nature of their instability, such as Twice-Spoken, Mirrorless, Hollow-Step, Afterimage, Wallpasser, or Seen-Then-Gone. A Veilborn may change names when their form shifts, when memories return, or when an old identity begins to fade beyond recovery.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, veilborn often become scouts, infiltrators, memory-readers, guides through anomaly zones, occult witnesses, couriers, and keepers of forgotten routes. Their unstable nature makes them difficult to track, difficult to confine, and unusually suited to work in places where normal bodies and records fail. Some serve underground networks as unseen messengers or wardens of abandoned passages, while others are coerced by corporations, governments, or occult orders seeking to weaponize their condition. A Veilborn’s value often comes from the same quality that makes others fear them: sometimes they are present exactly where no one expects presence to be possible. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Veilborn character brings training shaped by bodily stabilization, mutual aid, specialist medicine, and reliable personal records. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Veilborn reach adulthood around 20 years and commonly live 100 to 180 years, depending on how well their bodies remain stabilized. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Veilborn typically stand between 5'4" and 7'0". Their size is Medium.

Name: Veithyn
Intro: The Veithyn are dwellers of the shadowed border, a people shaped along the outer reaches of Nevermourn where darkness presses close to the mortal world without fully consuming it. They are neither creatures of pure shadow nor ordinary mortals, but something held in the dusk between presence and oblivion. Their grace is quiet, their movements unsettling, and their silence often feels like the pause before a storm. Outsiders fear them for their closeness to Nevermourn, but the Veithyn understand themselves as borderfolk: those who live where the dark begins, and who know which shadows must be watched.
Origin: Veithyn descend from Moeder,Mireborn. The Veithyn arose along the borderlands of Nevermourn, in regions where reality thins, light bends, and the surface of the Realm of Shadows brushes against the world without swallowing it whole. Their oldest settlements were built along rifts, black valleys, and twilight thresholds where other peoples could not remain for long. Legends speak of a silent pact with Nevermourn itself, an accord that allows the Veithyn to endure the dark without unraveling into it. Whether this pact was blessing, curse, ancestral bargain, or survival instinct refined over generations, the Veithyn still honor it through vigilance, restraint, and careful movement between worlds.
Appearance: Veithyn are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'8" to 6'6", with a tall, slender, and shadow-drawn build. They have an elongated and elegant skull, high and shadowed brows, a narrow and straight nose, and a narrow and elegant jaw. Their 2 abyss-dark eyes are commonly black, charcoal. Their skin is smoke-like and soft-edged, with charcoal to obsidian black coloration and visible black veins and blurred outline. Hair is smoke-like and drifting and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is quiet and slightly blurred. Typical grooming: Smoke-like hair left drifting or loosely bound without suppressing its motion. Common ornamentation includes dark layered garments, threshold charms, subdued metals, and watch-order insignia.
Culture & Identity: Veithyn culture centers on balance, vigilance, and the discipline of living beside an abyss without worshipping it. They do not view darkness as evil, but as a force that must be understood, negotiated with, and contained. Their settlements are often built from dark stone quarried near rifts, shaped into quiet halls, watchtowers, shadowed gardens, and sanctuaries where light is used sparingly and with intent. Many Veithyn belong to traditions such as Duskwardens, Shroudcallers, or Umbradancers, each reflecting a different relationship with the border: guarding it, studying it, or moving through it. Their greatest fear is forgetting that the dark is never merely beautiful. It is patient.
Societal Roles: Veithyn often become border wardens, scouts, shadow-mystics, rift watchers, night guides, infiltrators, scouts, occult scholars, and hunters of things that slip out of Nevermourn. Duskwardens patrol thresholds and ruined passes, ensuring that what belongs beyond the border remains there. Shroudcallers study shadow as substance, language, and warning, preserving lore that most peoples are too afraid to name. Umbradancers move between darkened spaces with unsettling speed, serving as messengers, spies, duelists, or assassins when necessity demands it. In mixed societies, Veithyn are often consulted when darkness behaves strangely, though few are invited to stay once the problem is solved.
Naming Conventions: Veithyn names are soft-spoken and weighted with history, often designed to be carried in whispers rather than proclaimed in crowds. Given names include Myris, Vaelith, Xiren, Tythos, Eska, Khaelis, Zoryn, Lysara, Veidra, Raleth, Nyra, and Osveth. Lineage names may preserve old houses or border settlements, such as Vaelros, Nythis, Kaeloris, Zhenkar, and Veyrith. Other surnames reference shadow, twilight, silence, or echoes, such as Duskwind, Nightborn, Shadowcrest, Emberveil, Ebonwhisper, Murkmourn, Silenshade, Blackrift, and Gloamwatch. A Veithyn may earn an additional title after surviving a crossing, sealing a breach, or returning from a place where their shadow did not follow.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Veithyn are rare but recognizable, usually found near old rifts, shadowed borders, Nevermourn-facing ruins, night markets, sealed archives, and cities built too close to places where light behaves incorrectly. They are treated with caution, reverence, or superstition depending on local history. Some serve as hired scouts, wardens, occult advisors, or guides through dangerous darklands, while others live quietly in twilight districts where lanterns are hooded and windows face away from the deepest shadows. Their presence often reassures those who understand their work and unsettles those who prefer to believe the border guards itself.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Veithyn, you bring restraint, grace, shadow, and the tension of belonging to the edge rather than the center. Decide how your character relates to Nevermourn: guardian, scholar, exile, hunter, mystic, or one who hears the dark calling more clearly than they admit. You may be calm, eerie, compassionate, severe, secretive, or quietly burdened by what you have seen beyond the light. Veithyn fit naturally as rogues, rangers, monks, warlocks, clerics, fighters, and wizards focused on stealth, darkness, perception, mobility, and liminal magic. Your strength is knowing that shadow is neither refuge nor enemy. It is a border, and borders must be kept.
Age: Veithyn reach adulthood around 25 years and commonly live 160 to 220 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Veithyn typically stand between 5'8" and 6'6". Their size is Medium.

Name: Velthask
Intro: Velthask are medium people descended from Vorthirith. Their communities are known for rank, public accountability, formal challenge, and the duties of leadership. Their unusual physical traits are treated as inherited anatomy, training concerns, or medical needs rather than as symbols detached from daily life.
Origin: Velthask descend from Vorthirith lineages that built hereditary courts and selected for large, durable bodies suited to command, ceremony, and protection. Their metallic carapace and cranial crests are inherited traits. Noble houses record descendance through birth ledgers, court titles, military service, and claims to old fortresses.
Appearance: Velthask are medium humanoids, typically standing 6'5" to 8'0", with a very tall, imposing, and rigid build. They have a crested and angular skull, armored and crest-integrated brows, an angular and integrated into carapace nose, and a narrow and armored jaw. Their 2 opaque gemstone-like eyes are commonly ruby, sapphire, onyx, amber, emerald. Their skin is polished metallic carapace, with petrified gold, brass, ivory, or electrum coloration and natural geometric carapace seams. Hair is absent, they bear metallic cranial crest and facial spines, and their secondary covering consists of natural metallic carapace. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is rigid and commanding. Typical grooming: Carapace polished, crests displayed, and every visible surface maintained ceremonially. Common ornamentation includes carapace-like formalwear, crowns, gemstone regalia, and rank-defining court symbols.
Culture & Identity: Velthask culture centers on rank, public accountability, formal challenge, and the duties of leadership. Children are trained through households, work groups, schools, or orders that teach the habits needed for their expected duties. Status is usually tied to reliable service, visible competence, and whether a person protects the community's records, routes, property, or people.
Societal Roles: Velthask most often become monarchs, magistrates, warlords, lawgivers, trade sovereigns, court arbiters, military patrons, oath-binders, and architects of political order. Some rule cities directly, while others dominate through contracts, vassalage, inherited claims, or carefully controlled commerce. Gilded Warlords assert authority through military excellence, Crowned Orators through law and declaration, and Ivory Regents through the sheer gravity of sovereign presence. Even those without a throne tend to organize the world around themselves, turning parties into courts, companies into retinues, and disputes into opportunities for recognition.
Naming Conventions: Velthask names are proclamations, meant to be spoken with weight and remembered as claims. Given names are bold, severe, and resonant, such as Xyrran, Daeloris, Tharnith, Vorzhal, Krymion, Veska, Myrthys, Yzra, Zhaelis, Oromis, Vhaldran, and Serathys. Lineage names often preserve dynastic force, such as Krynnith, Zalvrynn, Vhoris, Tyrmoth, Odrakhal, and Xalvorn. Title-names are common and may replace surnames entirely, especially after conquest, succession, or public victory. Examples include Crownforged, Stoneborne, Gildedblade, Ironregent, Thronewake, Lawmantle, Scepterhand, and Unbowed. A Velthask who loses status may be stripped of titles, a humiliation often treated as worse than injury.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, velthask most often become monarchs, magistrates, warlords, lawgivers, trade sovereigns, court arbiters, military patrons, oath-binders, and architects of political order. Some rule cities directly, while others dominate through contracts, vassalage, inherited claims, or carefully controlled commerce. Gilded Warlords assert authority through military excellence, Crowned Orators through law and declaration, and Ivory Regents through the sheer gravity of sovereign presence. Even those without a throne tend to organize the world around themselves, turning parties into courts, companies into retinues, and disputes into opportunities for recognition. They usually live near the workplaces, archives, roads, courts, clinics, fortifications, or workshops that support those duties.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Velthask character brings training shaped by rank, public accountability, formal challenge, and the duties of leadership. Decide which household, order, route, office, or settlement taught you, what obligation still follows you, and what practical problem forced you into the current adventure.
Age: Velthask reach adulthood around 40 years and commonly live 350 to 500 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Velthask typically stand between 6'5" and 8'0". Their size is Medium.

Name: Vessels
Intro: The Vessels are living constructs, designed rather than born, shaped by forgotten makers for war, labor, worship, knowledge, containment, or purposes no surviving tongue can name. They are relics that learned to continue after command ended. Some are elegant works of sacred craft, others crude frames of stone, metal, ceramic, bone, wood, or rune-bound flesh, but all carry a core of awareness that persists beneath damage, age, and abandonment. A Vessel was built to serve a function. What makes them dangerous, tragic, and remarkable is that many now choose to exist beyond it.
Origin: Vessels descend from Humans. Vessels were created by many hands across many ages: divine artisans, vanished empires, occult engineers, desperate war-priests, eldritch architects, and civilizations now known only through ruins. Some were soldiers made for wars that ended before memory. Some were caretakers for temples without worshippers. Others were built as archives, executioners, guardians, or prisons for things their makers lacked the courage to destroy. Their creators are gone, silent, or unwilling to claim them. What remains is the Vessel, walking through a world that may fear its construction while depending on the endurance it was given.
Appearance: Vessels are medium humanoids, typically standing 4'0" to 7'0", with a lean, assembled humanoid build. They have a blank humanoid mask skull, smooth or carved into mask brows, a minimal or artificial nose, and a hinged or sculpted jaw. Their 2 small, artificial eyes are commonly black, artificial, or absent. Their skin is patched composite, ceramic, or artificial flesh, with unpainted beige and grey coloration and maker marks, seams, repairs, and inscriptions. Hair is absent, they have minimal or absent ears, and their secondary covering consists of patched construct material. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with variable, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is neutral and mechanical. Typical grooming: Repairs maintained and often left visible as part of personal history. Common ornamentation includes maker marks, seals, cords, embedded relics, patched materials, and chosen identity symbols.
Culture & Identity: Vessels do not share a single culture, since they were made by different peoples for different reasons. Instead, they share a common question: what remains when purpose becomes choice? Some cling to their original function, finding comfort in duty. Others reject it entirely, treating obedience as the first prison they escaped. Many search for records of their makers, lost companions, unfinished commands, or the meaning of the core that grants them awareness. Among themselves, Vessels often show unusual patience, recognizing the difference between a tool, a relic, and a person can take centuries to understand.
Societal Roles: Vessels are often drawn, or pushed, into roles that echo their designs. Wardens become guards, sentinels, investigators, and protectors of forbidden places. Executioners become soldiers, bounty hunters, duelists, or feared champions. Oracles become scribes, witnesses, archivists, translators, and keepers of impossible memory. Binders become prison-keepers, curse-bearers, exorcists, or living seals. In Orimond, some are revered as holy relics, others studied as artifacts, and others hunted by those who want to disassemble, replicate, or command them. A Vessel’s usefulness is obvious. Their personhood is where the argument begins.
Naming Conventions: Vessel names are often adopted rather than inherited. Some use maker designations, serial marks, or sacred titles, such as Unit Seven, Gatehand, Reliquary-Third, Ash Warden, or Archive Vessel Ios. Others choose personal names after awakening to selfhood, favoring simple names that can be spoken without command, such as Cal, Oren, Sava, Thal, Iri, Voss, Nema, or Edrin. Many carry function-names or earned titles, such as Hollow Key, Bright Core, Stone Witness, Last Blade, Sealed Mercy, Iron Psalm, or Bell-Without-Tower. Changing one’s name is often a major act of self-definition, especially for a Vessel abandoning its original purpose.
Life in Orimond: Vessels appear in ruins, temples, battlefields, sealed archives, noble collections, mage laboratories, guild vaults, and city streets where no one can agree whether they are citizens, property, relics, or threats. Some serve openly as guards, scholars, laborers, or envoys, while others hide their cores from scavengers and artificers eager to harvest old craft. Institutions value them for endurance and memory, but that value often carries danger. A Vessel in Orimond must often prove they are more than the object others see. Every contract, friendship, and oath becomes a test of whether they are being recognized or merely used again.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Vessel, your story turns on purpose, autonomy, memory, and the uneasy boundary between tool and person. Decide what you were built for, whether that function still defines you, and what part of your design you fear others might activate, exploit, or misunderstand. You may be solemn, curious, dutiful, rebellious, gentle, terrifying, or quietly fascinated by ordinary life. Vessels fit naturally as fighters, paladins, artificers, clerics, warlocks, wizards, monks, and any character tied to endurance, lost history, ancient technology, divine craft, or sealed horrors. Your central question is simple: were you made to contain a soul, or did one grow inside you anyway?
Age: Vessels are fully formed when awakened and commonly live indefinitely while their core and body can be repaired. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Vessels typically stand between 4'0" and 7'0". Their size is Medium.

Name: Vexholmic
Slogan: Discipline is survival.
Quote: “Strength is trained. Mercy is chosen.”
Intro: Vexholmics are compact, heavily built humanoids raised in militarized communities. Their reinforced facial ridges, thick skin, and strong hands suit them to disciplined close combat and demanding physical work.
Origin: Vexholmics descend from Delvani populations selected and trained for fortress warfare over many generations. Their lineage retains Delvani density but developed a more angular frame, hardened nails, and reinforced facial structure.
Appearance: Vexholmic are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'0" to 6'0", with a compact, dense, and heavily built build. They have an angular and reinforced skull, reinforced and ridged brows, a broad and flattened nose, and a heavy and reinforced jaw. Their 2 small and predatory eyes are commonly silver, iron-blue, crimson, onyx. Their skin is thick and resilient, with ash, iron-grey, scarred red, or dark bronze coloration and reinforced brow ridges and cranial grooves. Hair is absent or very short, they have ridged pointed ears, and they bear reinforced brow and cheek ridges. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is braced and confrontational. Typical grooming: Minimal martial grooming; nails, ridges, and equipment kept combat-ready. Common ornamentation includes rank marks, battle titles, contract badges, disciplined armor, and earned weapon tokens.
Culture & Identity: Vexholmic culture values discipline, hierarchy, tactical competence, and reliability under pressure. Rank is earned through demonstrated ability and can be lost through negligence.
Societal Roles: Vexholmics commonly work as soldiers, mercenaries, bodyguards, tacticians, siege crews, drill instructors, fortress police, and military quartermasters. Civilian roles exist, but most are still organized around emergency readiness and chain of command.
Naming Conventions: Vexholmic names are short and forceful. Formal names often include unit, rank, battlefield title, or contract employer. A major earned title can replace a family name if the deed is recognized by commanders.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Vexholmics live in fortified districts, mercenary compounds, and barracks attached to powerful employers. Civilian communities still maintain regular drills and emergency plans.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Vexholmic character values preparation and proven competence. Decide who trained you, which command you regret obeying, and what responsibility matters more than rank.
Age: Vexholmic reach adulthood around 15 years and commonly live 60 to 80 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Vexholmic typically stand between 5'0" and 6'0". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Built for War|You have advantage on saving throws against being knocked prone or stunned.
Trait 2: Tactical Reflexes|Once per short or long rest, you can take the Dodge action as a bonus action.
Trait 3: Hardened Frame|You have resistance to nonmagical bludgeoning damage.
Trait 4: Combat Adaptation|Once per long rest, when you take damage, you gain temporary hit points equal to half your level (minimum 1).

Name: Vitrexin
Slogan: Gold flows where blood is refined
Quote: I am worth more drained than alive.
Intro: Vitrexin are human-descended people whose blood and tissues have been altered by alchemical compounds. Visible colored veins, sigils, and growths identify the substances carried in their bodies and often reveal how they were used or controlled.
Origin: Vitrexin descend from Humans subjected to hereditary alchemical alteration. Some lineages were created for medicine, labor, entertainment, or military use; others arose when escaped subjects had children and passed altered compounds to later generations.
Appearance: Vitrexin are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'0" to 6'4", with a lean and human-adjacent build. They have a human-like skull, human-like, sometimes sigil-marked brows, a human-like nose, and a human-like jaw. Their 2 human-like eyes are commonly human range; sometimes compound-bright. Their skin is human-like, with warm human tones coloration and visible colored sigils, veins, and compound marks. Hair is dark and tousled and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is upright. Typical grooming: Human-adjacent grooming adapted to protect or deliberately reveal compound markings. Common ornamentation includes colored sigils, coded jewelry, serial markers, reclaimed names, and concealed ownership marks.
Culture & Identity: Vitrexin culture is shaped by ownership records, escape networks, medical knowledge, and the recovery of personal identity. Communities share information about safe treatments, false documents, and organizations that exploit altered blood.
Societal Roles: Vitrexin work in every ordinary profession, but are often targeted as courtesans, controlled laborers, medical assets, performers, or sources of rare compounds.
Naming Conventions: Vitrexin may carry serial markers, compound codes, inherited names, or names reclaimed after escape. Choosing a personal name is an important act in many communities.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Vitrexin rely on discreet clinics, mutual-aid groups, and hidden routes that help them avoid trafficking and coercive contracts. Their legal status varies sharply between districts.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: A Vitrexin character knows that their body may be valuable to dangerous people. Decide which compound marks you carry, who helped you claim your identity, and whether you hide or display those marks.
Age: Vitrexin reach adulthood in their late teens and commonly live 80 to 140 years, with major variation caused by blood compounds and treatment. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Vitrexin typically stand between 5'0" and 6'4". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Engineered Circulation|Your blood carries a refined alchemical compound determined at character creation.
Trait 2: Marked Form|Your body bears visible physical markers identifying your blood compound which cannot be concealed except by magic.
Trait 3: Blood Extraction|As part of a short rest a creature can extract a vial of your blood with your consent or if you are incapacitated and you gain one level of exhaustion.
Trait 4: Chemical Resonance|Choose one blood profile and once per long rest gain its benefit such as Euphoria Focus Fury or Veil.
Trait 5: Tainted Recovery|You have disadvantage on saving throws against poison.

Name: Vok
Slogan: “The fire remembers.”
Quote: “Stone cracks. Steel bends. Fire endures.”
Intro: The Vok are dense, heat-bearing keepers of Cinderhold, a people whose bodies store warmth, steady flame, and release power with deliberate restraint. Though they bear traces of Dren ancestry, the Vok have become something distinct: not ash-walkers or hearth-keepers, but living vessels of furnace-heat, ember-current, and volcanic pressure. They are practical, grounded, and deeply intolerant of waste. Where the Cindralis shape fine works with patient hands, the Vok tend the deep fires and hidden flows that keep Cinderhold alive.
Origin: Vok descend from Dren. The Vok emerged from Dren-descended communities that settled near Cinderhold’s volcanic roots, forge-vaults, lava channels, and deep heat shrines. Generations of life among controlled flame, pressure-stone, conductive ores, and bound elemental currents changed them into natural heat vessels, able to gather, hold, and release thermal force through body and craft. Their ancestors endured fire as a condition of the world. The Vok learned to make fire into duty. Over time, they formed a close alliance with the Cindralis, who refine delicate works above while the Vok maintain the furnaces, vents, bellows, and emberworks below.
Appearance: Vok are medium humanoids, typically standing 5'8" to 7'0", with a dense, stocky, and muscular build. They have a strong humanoid skull, strong and metallic brows, a straight and flattened nose, and a firm and compact jaw. Their 2 narrow, glowing eyes are commonly molten gold, ember-orange, coal-red, storm-blue. Their skin is smooth metallic or volcanic, with coal-grey, dark steel, or volcanic black coloration and faint seams and pulse-lines. Hair is short, dark, and bristled and they have human-like ears. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is grounded and efficient. Typical grooming: Short practical grooming; vents, seams, and metallic hair kept clear of residue. Common ornamentation includes calibration marks, forge tokens, heat-safe tools, and symbols of maintained systems.
Culture & Identity: Vok culture centers on stability, efficiency, maintenance, and controlled release. Waste is treated as both practical failure and moral failing, whether in flame, metal, labor, speech, anger, or time. They respect systems that hold under pressure and people who improve what they inherit rather than merely complain about its flaws. Their communities are orderly and interdependent, with status earned through reliability, calibration, and calm during crisis. A Vok who keeps a forge-vault from bursting, a lava gate from failing, or a furnace district from freezing is honored more deeply than one who wins glory in a duel.
Societal Roles: Vok often become furnace-keepers, emberwrights, forge wardens, vent-stabilizers, lava-channel tenders, pressure-stone masons, elemental binders, smiths, kiln masters, and guardians of deep volcanic works. Pyroconductors specialize in flame handling, heated metals, and forge-side labor. Arcvolt Technicians become storm-channelers, rune-current readers, and handlers of lightning-bound devices. Magma-Bound Sentinels guard crater roads, deep heat wells, and unstable volcanic passages. In Cinderhold, the Vok are the quiet force beneath the city’s craft, keeping bellows breathing, lifts moving, gates sealed, baths warm, kilns steady, and foundries alive.
Naming Conventions: Vok names are firm, heavy, and structured, often carrying thermal, mineral, or craft associations. Given names include Jorrek, Durnis, Malrik, Vethan, Tormek, Xyra, Othiel, Rhenka, Talyis, Vezna, Korrun, Braska, and Voltan. Surnames and earned names often reference heat, ore, deep craft, or service to the furnaces, such as Heatbinder, Coilbrand, Emberforge, Voltshard, Ironcurrent, Gearstrike, Sparkgrind, Thermoset, Furnacehand, Deepcoil, Current-Keeper, and Ashcircuit. A Vok may gain a new name after calming a runaway forge, surviving an ember surge, repairing a deep vent, or saving a district from fire, smoke, or cold.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Vok are most common in Cinderhold, forge districts, volcanic settlements, mountain foundries, deep halls, and cities that depend on controlled heat for survival. They are respected for competence, though outsiders often mistake their economy of speech and motion for coldness. Vok quarters are warm, reinforced, and practical, organized around shared furnaces, stone vents, tool alcoves, heat baths, emergency shutters, and communal workshops. They are often called when a kiln fails, a mine grows too hot, a forge spirit becomes unstable, or a city’s hidden heatworks begin to falter. When the deep fires misbehave, people send for a Vok.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Vok, you bring discipline, technical insight, controlled force, and the belief that survival depends on systems that hold. Decide what kind of power your character understands best: forge-fire, volcanic pressure, storm-current, rune-craft, or the quiet authority of keeping others warm and safe. You may be pragmatic, stern, protective, dryly funny, proud, or obsessed with eliminating waste wherever you find it. Vok fit naturally as artificers, fighters, wizards, clerics, rangers, and any character focused on tools, fire, lightning, endurance, craft, or hazardous environments. Your strength is controlled output: knowing when to store heat, when to release it, and when to close the vents before everything burns.
Age: Vok reach adulthood around 25 years and commonly live 200 to 300 years. Visible aging varies by lineage, health, environment, and occupation.
Size Summary: Vok typically stand between 5'8" and 7'0". Their size is Medium.
Trait 1: Fire-Tempered|Resistance to fire damage.
Trait 2: Dren Legacy|Advantage on saving throws against exhaustion and environmental heat.
Trait 3: Forge Insight|Proficiency with smith’s tools or mason’s tools.
Trait 4: Living Furnace|Ignore penalties from extreme heat environments.
Trait 5: Enduring Frame|Once per long rest, reduce incoming damage by an amount equal to proficiency bonus.
Trait 6: Ember Memory|Advantage on checks related to ancient structures, forges, or volcanic sites.
CharGen: A character born Imperante whose inner directive precedes desire and shapes their choices.

Name: Vorraki
Slogan:
They built a world where we take orders.
We built a culture where we take care of our own.
Quote: “Strength without discipline is noise. Discipline without strength is a lie.”
Intro: The Vorraki are the shackled titans of Orimond, a people of fortified enclaves, disciplined armies, and controlled violence. Their nations are rarely vast, but they are feared far beyond their borders, for a Vorraki host can decide the fate of wars larger realms are too proud to fight honestly. They are soldiers, tacticians, shield-bearers, siege-breakers, and oath-bound contractors whose military strength is bought, leased, negotiated, or pledged under strict terms. They were not made to serve. They built societies where force is organized, priced, restrained, and remembered.
Origin: Vorraki descend from Dunhark. The Vorraki arose in harsh borderlands and contested marches where survival required more than strength. It required formations, supply lines, discipline, and the ability to turn limited territory into unbreakable defense. Over generations, Vorraki clans consolidated into small martial nations, fortress-states, and contract-holds whose value lay in trained force rather than fertile land or imperial reach. Their history is full of sieges, defensive pacts, mercenary charters, and wars fought under foreign banners for Vorraki gain. Outsiders often call this selling themselves. Vorraki call it sovereignty by other means.
Appearance: Vorraki are medium humanoids, typically standing 6'0" to 7'2", with a tall, broad, and powerfully muscular build. They have a broad and severe skull, heavy and severe brows, a broad or boar-like nose, and a broad with visible upper canines jaw. Their 2 deep-set and reflective eyes are commonly dark brown, grey, stone-like. Their skin is weathered, with olive-grey, charcoal, iron-brown, or deep bronze coloration and scars, campaign marks, and service tattoos. Hair is coarse; dark, braided, or locked, they have pointed ears, and they bear elongated upper canines. They have 2 arms and 2 legs, with humanoid proportions, five-fingered hands, and plantigrade feet. Their posture is tactical and upright. Typical grooming: Hair and facial hair worn in disciplined braids, locks, or military cuts. Common ornamentation includes campaign marks, service tattoos, battle honors, officer insignia, and oath-charter tokens.
Culture & Identity: Vorraki culture centers on martial sovereignty. They value discipline over fury, formation over individual glory, tactics over bravado, and national memory over personal vanity. Their societies are organized through war-councils, fortress houses, officer lineages, shield-companies, and oath-charters that define when, where, and for whom Vorraki force may be used. A contract is not a chain if it is negotiated from strength, and a campaign is not shameful if it feeds the hold, strengthens the people, and preserves independence. Uncontrolled violence is despised. War is a craft, and craft requires restraint.
Societal Roles: Vorraki often become professional soldiers, shield-captains, siege engineers, heavy infantry, war advisors, border wardens, military governors, fortress quartermasters, drillmasters, and commanders of leased companies. Their small nations often export military strength through carefully written charters, sending companies to fight in foreign wars while retaining command structures, cultural law, and rights of withdrawal. Some Vorraki become independent mercenary lords or military envoys, while others remain in their homeland training the next generation. In Orimond, to hire Vorraki is expensive. To betray their terms is catastrophic.
Naming Conventions: Vorraki names are short, hard, and practical, suited to shouted commands, shield-line calls, and campaign records. Given names include Blaze, Kray, Roka, Brunt, Vex, Daro, Strix, Maul, Tasha, Varn, Zera, Nok, Grath, Orra, and Korr. Company names and campaign titles often function as surnames, earned through deeds, defeats, victories, or formations served. Examples include Ironshade, Grudgeborn, Tenback, Deadright, Haulmark, Onechain, Gatehand, Bloodbrace, Stonejaw, Lastwall, Breakline, Red Phalanx, Black Standard, and Third Shield. A Vorraki may change titles after joining a new company, surviving a failed campaign, breaking a siege, or returning home with honor intact.
Life in Orimond: In Orimond, Vorraki are encountered as embassy guards, chartered companies, military advisors, fortress delegates, leased regiments, and disciplined foreign hosts camped outside city walls under negotiated terms. Their nations may be small, but few powers treat them lightly. Vorraki troops are hired to hold borders, break sieges, protect trade roads, suppress monsters, reinforce failing armies, or tip the balance in conflicts where local soldiers cannot be trusted. Their presence in a city is both reassurance and threat: someone has paid for order, and the terms of that order are written somewhere in Vorraki hands.
Playstyle & Roleplaying: As a Vorraki, you bring disciplined might, martial professionalism, and the politics of force under contract. Decide what hold, company, campaign, defeat, or charter shaped you, and whether you fight for homeland, coin, oath, honor, leverage, or survival. You may be protective, intimidating, strategic, blunt, honorable, ruthless, patriotic, or deeply suspicious of rulers who spend soldiers like loose copper. Vorraki fit naturally as fighters, paladins, barbarians, rangers, clerics, warlords, and any character focused on command, protection, intimidation, formation tactics, and controlled violence. Your strength is not only that you can fight. It is that your people made war into policy and survived by setting the terms.
